entry.title: Deep Dive: Essential Setup for S/4HANA and SAP Field Service Management Native Integration
entry.content:
Introduction

In today's fast-paced service landscape, the seamless flow of data between back-end ERP systems and front-line service operations is critical. The native integration between SAP S/4HANA and SAP Field Service Management (FSM) unlocks significant business value by enabling real-time master data synchronization, unified asset management, and end-to-end service order processing. By connecting these systems, organizations reduce manual data entry efforts, minimize errors, and ensure that field technicians have accurate, up-to-date information on equipment, customers, and spare parts.

This blog provides a deep technical walkthrough of the essential configuration steps required to set up this native integration. We will cover the complete landscape, starting from connectivity setup with Cloud Connector and authentication via OA2C, to configuring the Data Replication Framework (DRF), monitoring via the Application Interface Framework (AIF), setting up the Mobile Application Interface Framework (MAIF), and finalizing the FSM connector settings. Whether you are a technical consultant or an integration architect, this guide serves as a comprehensive reference for your implementation journey.

Integration Architecture Overview

The native integration leverages the SAP Business Technology Platform (BTP) as the integration middleware, enabling secure, bidirectional data flow between the on-premise S/4HANA system and the cloud-based Field Service Management solution. This architecture ensures robust connectivity through secure tunnels and utilizes standard, pre-built integration content.

Figure 1: Overview

Section 1: OA2C Configuration

OAuth 2.0 Client Credential Setup

This configuration is used for OAuth 2.0 client credential authentication setup between S/4HANA and SAP BTP services. It is a mandatory prerequisite before proceeding with other configurations.

Transaction Code: OA2C_CONFIG

Figure 2: OA2C Configuration page with FSM_PROFILE

 

Figure 3: Configure FSM_PROFILE with Client ID and Client Secret

Section 2: RFC Configuration

RFC (Remote Function Call) setup is the backbone of backend communication within the SAP landscape. Ensure that the RFC destinations are correctly configured to allow for seamless data exchange.

Figure 4: Create RFC of Type G

 

Figure 5: Enter the host name in RFC

Figure 6: Assign the OAuth Profile created in Section 1, to RFC

 Figure 7: Set the SSE Client PSE ID as Anonym SSL Client

Section 3: Cloud Connector Setup

The Cloud Connector serves as the secure tunnel between your on-premise S/4HANA system and the SAP BTP environment.

Access Link: https://<host>.cpi.c.eu-de-1.cloud.sap:8443/

  1. Log in to the Cloud Connector administration portal.
  2. Select Cloud to On-Premise from the menu to establish the tunnel mapping from SAP BTP to your S/4HANA backend system.
  3. Configure the internal host and port mapping to the virtual host and port.

Figure 8: Assign your cloud Connector in BTP

Figure 9: Cloud Connector Login Page

 

Figure 10: Select the BTP Sub Account

Figure 11: Configure Cloud to On-Premise with Hist Name and Authentication

Security Tip: 

Ensure that the system certificates are correctly exchanged between the Cloud Connector and the S/4HANA backend to prevent handshake errors during connectivity.

Section 4: SOAMANAGER Configuration

Transaction: SOAMANAGER

This configuration is critical for the setup of web services and is required for the following business objects:

  • Business Partner (BP)
  • BP Relationship
  • Product
  • Product Price
  • Product Stock

Figure 12: List of Objects that needs to be configured in SOAMANAGER

Configuration Steps:

  1. Create Manual Logical Port definitions for the consumer proxies.
  2. Set the newly created port as DEFAULT.
  3. Maintain technical user credentials for authentication.
  4. In the Transport Settings tab, enter the URL obtained after the iFlow deployment. This ensures that BP/SOAP events are correctly routed to Cloud Integration.

Figure 13: SOAMANAGER Home Screen: Select Web Service Configuration

Figure 14: In this example – Business Partner is configured

Figure 15: Create Manual Define logical ports and make the port as DEFAULT

 

Figure 16: Enter the Logical Port name

Figure 17: Enter technical user id for Authentication

Figure 18: Maintain the Messaging Tab as shown

Figure 19: Transport settings – mention the URL of obtained after iflow deployment

Note on iFlow URL

The iFlow URL must be obtained after deploying the relevant Integration Flow on SAP BTP Integration Suite. Configure this step after the endpoint is available.

Figure 20: End point URL of the IFLow after deployment

Section 5: Data Replication Framework (DRF)

DRF is the central tool used to distribute master and transactional data from S/4HANA to SAP FSM. It allows for flexible configuration of replication models and filters.

 Key Transactions

T-CodePurpose
DRFIMGCustomizing transaction to define replication models
DRFOUTTriggers the actual data replication (manual run)
DRFLOGViews logs to monitor data transfer status

 

Configuration Steps

  1. Define Business Systems: Navigate to create a new entry for the FSM target system

Figure 21: Define Business System

  1. Define Business Objects: Configure objects like Business Object 986 (Business Partner) and define what data entities should be replicated.

Figure 22: Define Business System

  1. Define Communication Channel: Set the Communication Channel to Replication via services.

Figure 23: Define Communication Channel

 

  1. Define Replication Models: Create a replication model that links the Business Object to the Target System.

Path - Data Replication -> Define Custom Settings for Data Replication -> Define Replication Models

  • Assign Outbound Implementation: Assign the relevant outbound implementation class to the model.

 

Figure 24: Assign Outbound Implementation

  • Assign Target Systems: Link the replication model to the specific FSM business system defined in step 1.

Figure 25: Assign Target Systems for Replication Model

  • Assign Outbound Parameters: Configure any additional parameters required for the replication logic.

Figure 26: Assign Outbound Parameter

  1. Activate the Replication Model: Finally, activate the model to enable live replication.

 

Figure 27: Activate Replication Model

 Testing Replication

Use T-Code   DRFOUT to manually trigger replication for testing purposes before scheduling background jobs. Use DRFLOG to inspect the results.

Section 6: AIF – SAP Application Interface Framework Monitoring

SAP AIF is used to monitor and manage integration messages between S/4HANA and FSM, providing a user-friendly interface for error handling.

Content Extraction

T-Code: /n /AIF/CONTENT_EXTRACT

 Extract the AIF content using the following scenario IDs:

Scenario IDIntegration Scope
SAP_AIF_0102Service Integration
SAP_AIF_0107Plant Maintenance Integration

Figure 28: Extract AIF Content

Viewing Loaded Content

Transaction Code - /n /AIF/CONTENT_DISP - Use this to view the loaded AIF Content after extraction.

Figure 29: Display extracted AIF Content

NOTE

Both SAP_AIF_01 02 and SAP_AIF_01 07 must be activated depending on your specific integration scope (Service vs. Plant Maintenance). Failure to activate the correct content will result in unmonitored messages.

 

Section 7: Customizing Configuration Values

This step involves maintaining specific configuration tables that control the behavior of the integration.

  • Maintain configuration tables with API details (Client ID, Client Secret, Auth URL).
  • Maintain DTO (Data Transfer Object) version settings to ensure compatibility between S/4HANA and FSM versions.
  • Save the configuration.

Figure 30: Integration Customizing Parameters

Figure 31: Maintain the parameter values as mentioned in help doc as a customizing entry

Section 8: Client Proxy Framework & Event Configuration

bgRFC Queue Setup

 Create bgRFC queues manually using T-Code SBGRFCCONF.  These queues are essential for handling background RFC processing for outbound events, ensuring that user transactions are not delayed by integration calls.

 

Figure 32 Maintain the bgRFC inbound destination manually

Section 9: Service Order Event Activation

Activate events in T-Code SWE2 . This configuration links the business object events (e.g., Service Order Created, Changed) to the receiver function modules that trigger the replication to FSM.

Figure 33: Activate events in SWE2

Troubleshooting Tip

Ensure all required event types are active in SWE2 before testing end-to-end replication. Missing event activations are a common root cause for failed replications where data changes in S/4HANA do not trigger any outbound message.

Section 10: MAIF – Mobile Application Interface Framework

MAIF is the framework required for replicating technical asset data. It is specifically used for the following object types:

  • Equipment
  • Functional Location
  • Characteristics
  • Classification

Configuration Steps:

  1. Activate BC Set: Use Transaction code SCPR20 to activate the BC Set /ACI/ASSET_CENTRAL_INTEGRATION and CRMS4_FSM_INTEGRATION_REF.
  2. Verify MDO Configuration: Navigate to Tcode -/n/SYCLO/CONFIGPANEL and verify that the Master Data Object (MDO) entries exist for the asset objects.Figure 34: Check if BC Set is activated
  3. Admin & Monitoring: - Use T-Code /n/SYCLO/ADMIN to maintain to maintain integration parameter details like API details, DTO versions etc.

    Figure 35: Maintain integration parameters in Additional Properties of Server Management

    Figure 36: Check Log in Inbound transaction Monitoring

Section 11: FSM Connector Settings (FSM → S/4HANA Inbound)

FSM Admin Portal

URL: https://<Cluster>.fsm.cloud.sap/admin

Routing iFlow

Configure the Routing iFlow in SAP BTP Integration Suite. This iFlow acts as the entry point from FSM to CPI to receive FSM event payloads. Events triggered in FSM (e.g., service confirmation, time recording) are received here and routed back to S/4HANA.

Figure 37: FSM Connector Configuration

 

Figure 38: Additional Configurations for different Objects

Error Monitoring & Reprocessing

Use the FSM Connector Error Monitoring tool for tracking failed inbound messages. This interface allows administrators to view error details and reprocess failed messages directly from the monitoring console.

Figure 39: FSM Connector Error Monitoring and Reprocessing

Conclusion

 

This blog covered the complete technical setup landscape for native S/4HANA-FSM integration, from authentication and connectivity (OA2C, Cloud Connector) through data replication frameworks (DRF, MAIF) to monitoring tools (AIF). Following these configuration steps ensures a robust, production-ready integration that supports seamless data consistency across your service operations.

As next steps, we recommend testing end-to-end scenarios—from master data replication to service order execution—while monitoring the data flow using AIF and FSM connector tools. Additionally, always keep your integration content updated by leveraging SAP's continuous updates to the BTP Integration Suite packages.

References & Useful Links

  • SAP Help Portal – Field Service Management Integration

entry.title: CI MCP Server: Managing SAP Cloud Integration with AI Assistants
entry.content:
Introduction

SAP Cloud Integration (part of SAP Integration Suite) has a comprehensive OData V2 API that covers everything from integration content management to message monitoring, security material, and partner directory operations. But working with these APIs typically means writing scripts, using Postman, or navigating the Web UI manually.

What if you could simply ask your AI assistant: "Show me all failed messages from the last hour" or "Deploy the latest version of my iFlow"? That's what the CI MCP Server enables.

Built on the odata-mcp-proxy npm package, the CI MCP Server exposes all 32 entity sets of the SAP CPI OData V2 API as MCP tools,  with zero custom code. Read more about the odata-mcp-proxy here: https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-blog-posts-by-members/odata-mcp-proxy-introduction/ba-p/14348684

What Can You Do With It?

The CI MCP Server gives AI assistants complete programmatic access to your SAP Cloud Integration tenant:

Integration Content Lifecycle

  • Create, read, update, and delete integration packages
  • Manage iFlows, value mappings, message mappings, and script collections
  • Track build and deploy status
  • View runtime artifact versions

Message Monitoring

  • Query message processing logs with rich filtering (status, date range, sender, receiver)
  • Drill into error details, attachments, and adapter attributes
  • Inspect custom header properties
  • Check exactly-once processing (ID mapping and idempotent repository)

Data Stores & Variables

  • Browse and delete data store entries
  • Inspect runtime variables
  • Monitor JMS queues (broker capacity, queue depth)

Security Material

  • Manage keystore entries, certificates, and SSH keys
  • CRUD operations on user credentials and OAuth2 client credentials
  • Manage secure parameters and certificate-to-user mappings
  • Configure access policies with artifact references

Partner Directory

  • Full CRUD on partners, string/binary parameters
  • Manage alternative partners and authorized users

API Categories & Entity Sets

The 32 entity sets are organized into 6 categories:

Category

Entity Sets

Description

integration-content

8

Packages, artifacts, build status, custom tags

message-processing-logs

3

Message logs with 5 navigation properties

message-stores

6

Data stores, variables, JMS queues, number ranges

log-files

2

Runtime logs and log archives

security-content

8

Keystores, credentials, certificates, access policies

partner-directory

5

Partners, parameters, authorized users

You can selectively enable categories using the ENABLED_API_CATEGORIES environment variable. For example, to only expose monitoring tools:

ENABLED_API_CATEGORIES=message-processing-logs,log-files

Configuration

The entire server is defined in ci-api-config.json:

{
  "server": {
    "name": "ci-mcp-server",
    "version": "1.0.0",
    "description": "SAP Cloud Integration MCP Server"
  },
  "apis": [{
    "name": "cpi",
    "destination": "CPI_DESTINATION",
    "pathPrefix": "/api/v1",
    "csrfProtected": true,
    "entitySets": [
      {
        "entitySet": "IntegrationPackages",
        "description": "Integration packages ...",
        "category": "integration-content",
        "keys": [{ "name": "Id", "type": "string" }],
        "operations": { "list": true, "get": true, "create": true, "update": true, "delete": true },
        "filterableProperties": ["Id", "Name", "Vendor", "Mode", "Version"]
      },
      ... (32 entity sets total)
    ]
  }]
}

Key configuration choices:

  • csrfProtected: true — SAP CPI uses OData V2 with CSRF token protection. The proxy automatically fetches and manages CSRF tokens for all mutating operations (POST, PATCH, DELETE).
  • Single destination — All 32 entity sets share one CPI tenant destination with the /api/v1 path prefix.
  • Navigation properties — MessageProcessingLogs has 5 navigation properties (Attachments, ErrorInformations, AdapterAttributes, CustomHeaderProperties, MessageStoreEntries), each generating an additional _list tool for drilling into related data.

Setting Up the BTP Destination

Create a destination in your SAP BTP Cockpit:

Property

Value

Name

CPI_DESTINATION

Type

HTTP

URL

https://<tenant>.it-cpi018.cfapps.<region>.hana.ondemand.com

Authentication

OAuth2ClientCredentials

Client ID

From your CPI service key

Client Secret

From your CPI service key

Token Service URL

https://<subdomain>.authentication.<region>.hana.ondemand.com/oauth/token

To get the service key credentials, create a service instance of "Process Integration Runtime" with plan "api" in your BTP subaccount, then create a service key.

Local Development

For local testing without BTP, set environment variables:

CPI_DESTINATION_BASE_URL=https://tenant.it-cpi018.cfapps.eu10.hana.ondemand.com
CPI_DESTINATION_TOKEN_URL=https://subdomain.authentication.eu10.hana.ondemand.com/oauth/token
CPI_DESTINATION_CLIENT_ID=your-client-id
CPI_DESTINATION_CLIENT_SECRET=your-client-secret

Or use the default-env.json with the destination from BTP:

{
  "VCAP_SERVICES": {
    "xsuaa": [
      {
        "name": "mcp-uaa",
        "label": "xsuaa",
        "tags": ["xsuaa"],
        "credentials":<btp xssuaa credentials>
      }
    ],
    "destination": [
      {
        "name": "mcp-dest",
        "label": "destination",
        "tags": ["destination"],
        "credentials":<btp destination credentials>
      }
    ],
    "connectivity": [
      {
        "name": "mcp-conn",
        "label": "connectivity",
        "tags": ["connectivity"],
        "credentials": <btp connectivity credentials>
      }
    ]
  }
}

Then run:

npm install
npm start

Deploying to SAP BTP

# Build MTA archive
npm run build:btp

# Deploy to Cloud Foundry
npm run deploy:btp

The MTA deployment creates three service instances (Destination, Connectivity, XSUAA) and binds them to your application. The health check endpoint at /health is used by Cloud Foundry to monitor application status.

Example Conversations

Here are some example prompts you can use with AI assistants:

User: "Show me all integration packages"
AI: Calls IntegrationPackages_list

User: "What iFlows are in the SAP_HCI_Monitoring package?"
AI: Calls IntegrationPackages_IntegrationDesigntimeArtifacts_list
    with path: "('SAP_HCI_Monitoring')"

User: "Show me failed messages from the last 24 hours"
AI: Calls MessageProcessingLogs_list
    with path: "?$filter=Status eq 'FAILED' and LogEnd gt datetime'2026-03-11T00:00:00'"

User: "What's the error for message ID abc123?"
AI: Calls MessageProcessingLogs_ErrorInformations_list
    with path: "('abc123')"

Project Structure

ci-mcp-server/
├── ci-api-config.json      # All 32 entity set definitions
├── package.json            # Single dependency: odata-mcp-proxy
├── mta.yaml                # BTP Cloud Foundry deployment
├── xs-security.json        # XSUAA roles (Viewer/Editor/Admin)
└── default-env.json        # Local dev credentials (gitignored)

Links

  • CI MCP Server (GitHub): https://github.com/lemaiwo/ci-mcp-server
  • OData MCP Proxy (npm): https://www.npmjs.com/package/odata-mcp-proxy
  • OData MCP Proxy (GitHub): https://github.com/lemaiwo/odata-mcp-proxy
  • SAP CPI OData API Reference: SAP Business Accelerator Hub

entry.title: DEIP — Domain Enterprise Integration Pattern: A Semantic Control Plane for Enterprise Integration
entry.content:
Disclaimer

Between March 12 and 13, 2026, during a late-night architecture review, the model I had been developing finally crystallized.
What had initially started as two SAP-oriented patterns converged into a five-layer semantic integration architecture, culminating in

DEIP – Domain Enterprise Integration Pattern.

On February 7, 2026 the scope of this work was limited to two patterns designed for SAP landscapes:

  • DCRP – Domain-Centric Routing Pattern
  • PDCP – Package Domain-Centric Pattern

Both focused on solving structural governance problems in SAP API Management and SAP Cloud Integration.

In just 36 days this initial nucleus evolved into a fully formalized five-layer semantic control architecture, designed to address a problem that many integration architects recognize but rarely formalize: integration sprawl.

This problem is not new.

For 17 years I have observed the same structural pattern repeating itself across different generations of SAP integration technologies:

  • SAP XI – fragmented Software Components
  • SAP PI/PO – interface and configuration proliferation
  • SAP BTP Integration Suite – proxy, package, credential and artifact sprawl inside CPI landscapes
  • SAP API Management - Proxy Sprawl Apocalypse
  • EDA and SOLACE in the same landscape for event mesh, what ? Serio ?
  • Others.....

Each generation improved the technology stack, but the structural indexing problem remained the same: integrations were organized by system or project, rather than by domain intent.

During these 36 days of research and experimentation, this recurring chaos was distilled into a single architectural model.

At the center of this model sits DEIP, the parent architecture layer responsible for governing the three execution patterns:

DEIP — Semantic Control Plane Decision Model
└── Decision Pipeline
       → Intent
       → Interaction Type
       → Quality Profile
       → Integration Capability
       → Protocol Profile
       → Platform Binding
    └── SDIA — Semantic Domain Integration Architecture
           (DEIP Reference Architecture)
        ├── GDCR — Gateway Domain-Centric Routing
        │        Reference Implementation — recommended starting point
        ├── DDCR — Domain Driven-Centric Router
        │        Routing and capability resolution engine
        └── ODCP — Orchestration Domain Composition Pattern
                 Cross-domain orchestration layer
     └── Execution Platforms

    SAP BTP · AWS · Azure · Kong · Kafka · IoT · AI/LLM · etc.

Together, these patterns form a domain-centric semantic control plane that sits above any specific execution platform.

From this point of closure — looking at SAP landscapes where the required capabilities already exist but are often applied without consistent domain semantics — this final blog post of the series begins.

Disclaimer SAP

SAP BTP Integration Suite already provides all the capabilities necessary to implement and validate this architecture in practice.

The platform offers:

  • a mature API Management layer
  • a powerful Cloud Integration runtime
  • Event Mesh
  • security services
  • and strong governance tooling.

The problem has never been the SAP platform itself.

Instead, the issue often lies in how integrations are indexed and governed.Many landscapes organize integrations by projects and systems, rather than by domain intent, which leads to proxy, package and credential sprawl even on top of a strong platform.

This same concern was raised earlier in January 2026 in my SAP Community article:

 Clean Architecture vs The Frankenstein Landscape

This blog originated from a situation of chaos that I observed in several customer landscapes.

The SAP BTP Integration Suite was available, but the way integration responsibilities were distributed made the architecture unnecessarily fragmented.

In some environments the pattern looked like this:

  • SAP API Management used only for standard APIs

  • SAP Cloud Integration (CPI) used only for standard packages

  • MuleSoft API-Led used for custom APIs

  • Boomi acting as a cloud middleware for custom integrations

And that was only part of the picture.

In many landscapes additional platforms were also introduced for other integration styles:

  • Event Mesh / Event Brokers

  • Kafka

  • IBM MQ

  • IoT platforms

  • Queue-based middleware

  • Streaming platforms

Each new integration requirement often resulted in another platform, another gateway, or another integration layer.

When I saw these architectures repeating themselves across different customers, a simple question came to my mind:

Is this really serious architecture?

Does this actually make sense from an integration perspective ? 

A kind of Frankenstein landscape built from overlapping platforms and responsibilities?

For me, the answer was no.

In most cases this situation is not driven by real technical constraints.
It is usually the result of organizational fragmentation, vendor tendency, or what I call “architects by manual” — architects who simply follow platform playbooks instead of questioning the architecture itself.

In many cases the result looks like multiple platforms overlapping while performing essentially the same functions.

The landscape becomes fragmented across different technologies and integration styles: APIs, events, queues, messaging platforms and middleware layers. What should be a coherent integration architecture slowly turns into a patchwork of tools and responsibilities.

This creates several structural problems.

There is multi-protocol exposure without clear consolidation, where the same business capability appears through different protocols such as REST, SOAP, OData, BAPI, RFC or IDoc, often managed by separate proxies and platforms.

At the same time, there is no centralized governance for integration decisions. Each team chooses tools, protocols or patterns independently, which leads to architectural inconsistency.

The consequences are predictable:

  • overlapping platforms performing the same functions

  • lack of centralized governance

  • high maintenance cost

  • complex operational landscapes

  • fragmented teams with different platform specializations

  • limited collaboration around the business domain itself

Instead of organizing integration around the domain, the architecture becomes organized around tools and vendors.

From my perspective, this is where the real problem begins.

When I look at these landscapes today, after studying integration structures more deeply, I often see something that resembles an architectural collapse in slow motion. Too many tools, too many integration surfaces, and too little structural coherence.

That realization pushed me to start researching this problem more seriously.

I kept studying, experimenting, and trying to understand how integration architecture could be organized from a more fundamental perspective.

One of the questions that kept coming back was simple:

Do we really need six different proxies for the same capability?

If the same business capability is exposed through multiple protocols – for example REST, SOAP and OData – does it really need to result in separate integration surfaces every time? Or can we centralize these capabilities behind a smaller number of domain‑driven HTTPS entry points, instead of multiplying proxies and integration layers across platforms ? 

And if we look one level deeper, the same question applies to the underlying protocols themselves.

Why do we need so many protocols for the same domain behaviour? In many cases, BAPIs, RFCs and IDocs can be exposed as HTTPS web services (REST, SOAP or OData) behind a single semantic façade.

Centralizing domain capabilities over HTTPS does not remove backend diversity, but it drastically reduces the number of exposed surfaces and makes the integration landscape governable again.

The same question applies to SAP Cloud Integration.

Do we really need dozens of CPI packages and technical users for the same domain?

If one business domain is implemented across multiple backends – SAP S/4HANA, Salesforce, legacy ERPs, logistics providers – does it really require a separate CPI package and a new technical user for every single interface and project?

Or can we consolidate these flows into a small number of domain‑centric packages, with tiered technical users aligned to sub‑domains, instead of multiplying packages and credentials until the landscape becomes ungovernable?

These questions eventually led me to formalize the architecture that I now call:

DEIP — Domain Enterprise Integration Pattern.

DEIP was designed precisely to address this type of architectural fragmentation by starting from the domain, rather than from the technology or the platform.

From there, the integration architecture can be derived in a deterministic way.

My research is not perfect and it is still evolving, but the goal is simple: to provide a starting point for bringing structural coherence back into integration architectures.

Wherever I work, that is the direction I intend to follow — organizing integration around the domain, not around fragmented platforms.

As a way of acknowledging the SAP ecosystem that shaped much of this work over the past 17 years, I am making a small professional commitment.

Based on my experience working with several API management platforms – including five different API gateways during the research – SAP API Management stands out for its user interface, configuration simplicity and proxy creation experience.

However, an important realization emerged during this research: when a  DDCR (Domain Driver Centric Router) is introduced, the role of the API gateway itself changes significantly.

At that point, the gateway is no longer just a platform for exposing and managing URLs.

It becomes part of a metadata‑driven control plane, where the final destination is resolved deterministic dynamically from domain intent, metadata and lightweight routing logic.In practical terms, this means that any API management platform – regardless of vendor – can operate at the same architectural level when combined with DDCR.

The gateway stops being merely a URL manager and becomes a decoupling layer between intent and destination, resolved through metadata and deterministic routing.


In this model, SAP API Management evolves from a traditional API gateway into a domain‑driven integration entry point, where routing decisions are driven by the control plane rather than statically defined inside individual proxies.

Alignment with SAP Strategic Directions

This work aligns directly with three strategic directions already promoted by SAP:

  • Clean Core
    • DEIP keeps protocol complexity and integration decision logic outside of S/4HANA by moving these concerns into a semantic control plane on SAP BTP.
    • This allows the core ERP to remain standard while integrations stay governed and evolvable.
  • RISE with SAP
    • By indexing integrations on domain intent rather than on individual systems, DEIP provides RISE transformation programs with a structured way to rationalize legacy interfaces during cloud migrations.
  • ISA-M
    • DEIP complements ISA-M by operating as a decision layer above the integration scenario model.While ISA-M classifies integration scenarios, DEIP maps them to capabilities, protocol profiles and platform bindings, ensuring that integration architectures remain domain-driven and consistent across platforms.

Dedication and Acknowledgement

Dedication

This work is dedicated to my father, Brigadier Ricardo Holanda Viana,
The Commander, whose discipline, courage and leadership shaped much of the character behind this journey.

Acknowledgement

Beyond its personal dedication, this work stands on the shoulders of foundational contributions that have shaped modern enterprise architecture and integration thinking.

Key references that served as intellectual guidance in the development of this model include:

  • Hohpe & Woolf (2003) — Enterprise Integration Patterns

  • Martin Fowler (2002) — Patterns of Enterprise Application Architecture

  • Eric Evans (2003) — Domain-Driven Design

  • Sam Newman (2015) — Building Microservices

  • Kreps et al. (2011) — Kafka: A Distributed Messaging System for Log Processing

  • Istio Team (2017) — Service Mesh control plane architectures

  • Zhamak Dehghani (2022) — Data Mesh

These works served as conceptual references and navigational points during the research and development process.

The central motivation behind this work was to revisit these architectural patterns and integration models and explore how they could be structurally combined into a coherent enterprise architecture perspective, organized around a single primary key:

the intent of the domain.

In this model, domain intent becomes the organizing principle that allows architectural decisions — from interaction patterns to protocol profiles and platform bindings — to be navigated consistently across the enterprise integration landscape.

Ricardo Luz Holanda Viana - March 2026

Introduction

Enterprise integration landscapes have evolved significantly over the past two decades.
Technologies have improved, platforms have matured, and new capabilities have been added to integration stacks across the industry.

Yet one structural problem persists across almost every enterprise landscape: integration sprawl.

Interfaces are frequently indexed by systems, projects, or technical artifacts, rather than by the intent of the domain they serve. Over time this leads to landscapes filled with proxies, packages, credentials, endpoints and flows that are technically functional, but structurally difficult to understand, govern and evolve.

This blog introduces DEIP — Domain Enterprise Integration Pattern, a vendor-agnostic semantic meta-framework structured across five architectural levels, governed entirely as a metadata control plane, designed to bring structural clarity to modern enterprise integration landscapes.

At its core, DEIP proposes a simple but powerful shift:
integration decisions should begin with domain intent, not with technology.

From that starting point, integration architectures can be navigated through a structured decision pipeline that determines:

  • the interaction type

  • the required quality characteristics

  • the integration capability

  • the protocol profile

  • and finally the platform binding.

This model forms a semantic control plane that governs runtime architectures such as SDIA, GDCR, DDCR and ODCP, while remaining independent from any specific execution platform.

The goal of this article is not to dive into every technical detail of the model.
Instead, it aims to provide architects with a clear conceptual overview of the architecture and the problem it addresses.

A complete technical companion document — expected in the coming months — will describe in detail how to map domains, structure the decision model, and navigate the architectural layers in real enterprise landscapes.

Until then, the most important idea to retain is simple:

everything begins with a single primary key: the intent of the domain. - "The Domains never lies"


Interpretation Note

This timeline should not be interpreted as positioning DEIP as “higher or better” to the foundational work of Hohpe & Woolf (2003).

Enterprise Integration Patterns defined 65 fundamental patterns describing how messages move through enterprise integration systems. Their work remains one of the most important references in the field and continues to guide integration architecture today.

The name Domain Enterprise Integration Pattern (DEIP) was deliberately chosen as a tribute to that work. Hohpe and Woolf defined the patterns that govern how messages travel inside enterprise integration systems.

DEIP builds on that legacy by addressing a different architectural question that emerged as enterprise landscapes evolved into multi-cloud, multi-vendor environments and AI-driven systems.

While Enterprise Integration Patterns describe how messages move across integration infrastructure, DEIP proposes a structural model for how domain intent moves across the enterprise landscape.

In this model, the domain becomes the primary architectural key, allowing integration decisions to be navigated consistently across interaction types, capabilities, protocol profiles and platform bindings.

In that sense, DEIP should be understood as an evolutionary architectural layer built on top of the foundations established by Enterprise Integration Patterns, extending their ideas into modern enterprise integration landscapes.

This work is therefore presented not as a replacement of those foundations, but as a continuation of them — an attempt to take the principles established in 2003 and structure them for the next generation of enterprise integration architectures.

What is DEIP?

DEIP – the Domain Enterprise Integration Pattern – is a semantic decision architecture designed to bring structural clarity to modern enterprise integration landscapes. Instead of organizing integrations by systems, projects or technical artifacts, DEIP proposes that every integration decision starts from a different primary key: the intent of the domain. In this model, the domain becomes the organizing principle used to navigate integration decisions across the entire landscape, and a semantic control plane replaces ad‑hoc design inside individual flows, proxies or API configurations.

The DEIP Decision Pipeline (6 stages)

At the center of DEIP is a six‑stage decision pipeline:

Intent → Interaction Type → Quality Profile → Integration Capability → Protocol Profile → Platform Binding  

Each stage answers a different question and progressively transforms domain intent into a concrete integration implementation:

  • Intent – What is the semantic purpose of the integration?
    Examples: invoice.read.finance, orders.create.ecommerce, iot.sensor.stream, security.identity.authenticate.

  • Interaction Type – Which interaction family does it belong to?
    Examples: request, command, event, stream, batch, workflow, replication.

  • Quality Profile – What non‑functional characteristics are required?
    Examples: low latency, high throughput, at‑least‑once delivery, strict ordering, strong authentication, data sovereignty.

  • Integration Capability – Which abstract integration capability is needed?
    Examples: API mediation, event streaming, CDC replication, long‑running workflow, IoT ingestion, AI tool routing, security authentication/authorization.

  • Protocol Profile – Which concrete protocol or messaging style implements that capability?
    Examples: HTTP/REST, gRPC, OData, Kafka, MQTT, AMQP, ETL/CDC, OpenID Connect.

  • Platform Binding – On which runtime platform will this flow execute?
    Examples: SAP API Management, SAP Cloud Integration, SAP Event Mesh, Kafka cluster, IoT broker, identity provider, Kubernetes/Istio.

By navigating these seven stages, architects can consistently determine how integrations should be implemented, independent of specific vendors or products.

Metadata as the Control Mechanism

DEIP does not introduce another integration product. It operates purely as a semantic control plane expressed as metadata that describes the integration landscape. This metadata acts as the control map of the architecture and can live in a structured database, a metadata catalog, a governance repository, or—even in the early stages—a well‑structured spreadsheet.

Metadata control plane = a table where you store your enterprise integration decisions.

Can be:
- Excel          ← it's metadata
- Google Sheets  ← it's metadata
- Database       ← it's metadata
- Redis / KVM    ← it's metadata
- Sticky note on the wall ← technically also metadata

The fancy name is just that — a table where you store the map of intentions.

The sophistication is not WHERE you store it. It's WHAT you store and how DDCR uses it.

A simple starting point can look like this:

Domain                 Intent                     Interaction         Capability                     Protocol           Platform

OrdersCreate OrderAsync, eventEvent publicationKafkaAWS
CustomersUpdate ProfileSync APIRequest/ResponseRESTSAP BTP
PaymentsPayment ConfirmationEvent (notification)Event notificationAMQPAzure
IoTSensor Telemetry IngestionStreamTelemetry stream ingestionMQTT → KafkaIoT Hub + Kafka/K8s
IoTDevice Firmware UpdateCommandReliable device commandMQTT QoS 1IoT Hub
ServiceProvision Application SpaceAsync workflowProvisioning orchestrationREST + WebhookSAP BTP
ServiceRestart Critical ServiceCommandOperational runbookgRPC/RESTKubernetes / Istio
SecurityUser AuthenticationRequestAuthentication serviceOpenID ConnectIdentity Provider
SecurityThreat Detection AlertEvent (alert)Security event routingHTTPS / JSONSIEM
SecurityCredential RotationBatch / workflowSecret lifecycle managementAPI / CLIVault / KMS
 
Over time, this table evolves into a living architectural map of the enterprise integration landscape, where each row is a governed decision path through the DEIP pipeline.

From Decision Model to Runtime Architecture

DEIP itself is not a runtime system. It is an architectural decision model that governs runtime architectures such as:

  • SDIA – Semantic Domain Integration Architecture

  • GDCR – Gateway Domain‑Centric Routing

  • DDCR – Domain‑Driven Centric Router

  • ODCP – Orchestration Domain Composition Pattern

These patterns implement the decisions derived from the DEIP pipeline inside real integration platforms (for example, SAP API Management, SAP Cloud Integration, Event Mesh, IoT brokers, service meshes and identity providers). The semantic control plane decides what should happen and where; the runtime patterns execute those decisions.

The core principle behind all of this remains intentionally simple and universal:
every integration decision starts from a single primary key — the intent of the domain.

DEIP and DDCR — A Simple Mathematical Model

Although DEIP is primarily an architectural decision model, its pipeline can also be expressed as a simple mathematical function that transforms domain intent into a concrete integration implementation.

Friends from SAP, I’m going to present the mathematical formulas that I derived. But don’t worry — the solution is DEIP itself. The formulas are only here as a visual aid to help understand the model.

What matters is the architectural, structural and execution vision behind it. At this point, the way I see integration architecture is very simple: I see patterns, domains and mathematics.

Mathematics is universal - 2 + 2 = 4 anywhere in the world.

The DEIP pipeline is a sequence of deterministic functions. Each stage is a formal reduction where context constrains the valid outputs:

f₁ : I × C → T
Intent + Context → Interaction Type

f₂ : I × T × C → Q
Intent + Interaction Type + Context → Quality Profile

f₃ : I × T × Q → Cap
Intent + Interaction Type + Quality Profile → Integration Capability

f₄ : Cap → Pprot
Integration Capability → Protocol Profile

f₅ : Pprot × C → Pplat
Protocol Profile + Context → Platform Binding

In this model:

I     = Domain Intent
C     = Context (environment, constraints, policies)
T     = Interaction Type
Q     = Quality Profile
Cap   = Integration Capability
Pprot = Protocol Profile
Pplat = Platform Binding

 

DDCR Resolution Model - Remember in SAP APIM it's just the javascript policy the name is my creation just it.

Once the decision pipeline produces a platform binding, the DDCR engine resolves the final runtime configuration using deterministic key resolution.

{ f(key) → value }

Where the key represents the resolved integration identity derived from the DEIP pipeline, and the value represents the runtime configuration to be applied (routing target, protocol adapter, credentials, endpoint, or orchestration behavior).

Look at the table below for example. The Order to Cash process is essentially the same anywhere in the world.

So how does DEIP interpret this?

No matter where you are in the world, regardless of language or platform, the entry point for any Order to Cash call is:

/<domain>/<entity>/<action>/<target>

Example:

/sales/order/create

What does this mean inside SAP API Management ?

Only one proxy is required. It can be defined using a single OpenAPI or Swagger specification with an entry point like:

/sales/orders/create/<target>

The routing is static, and a JavaScript policy (DDCR) resolves the final destination.

This means you can place 20 vendors behind a single API proxy in SAP API Management.

The code can include custom headers for tracking, and additional headers can also be added if needed.

This approach eliminates proxy sprawl at the root.

That is the idea behind DDCR.

In practice, the DDCR runtime proved this approach works. A simple JavaScript policy implementation processed around 2,067,904 requests across 8 platforms and 5 programming languages, running continuously for about 29 hours, withzero routing failures — 158 network-layer failures only (ECONNRESET/ETIMEDOUT)

The full implementation details, experiments and runtime evidence are available in the GitHub repository associated with this work.

 

DEIP(Intent, Context) → Implementation

 

Applying DEIP in Practice

Once the DEIP decision pipeline is defined, the next step is applying its outcomes inside the runtime architecture.

DEIP itself does not execute integrations.
Instead, it acts as a semantic control plane that guides how runtime components should behave.

In practice, the pipeline outputs are implemented through the SDIA architecture and its execution patterns.

DEIP → SDIA

The first layer where DEIP becomes operational is SDIA (Semantic Domain Integration Architecture).

SDIA represents the runtime architecture organized by domain, where integrations are grouped and governed according to their domain intent.

Instead of organizing integration artifacts by systems or projects, SDIA structures them by domains, such as:

Orders
Payments
Customers
Logistics
Notifications

Each domain becomes a semantic boundary where integration decisions derived from the DEIP pipeline are applied consistently.

SDIA → GDCR

At the gateway layer, DEIP decisions guide GDCR (Gateway Domain-Centric Routing).

GDCR is responsible for routing requests according to the domain intent.

Instead of routing based only on technical endpoints, GDCR uses the domain structure to determine where requests should flow.

Example:

/<domain>/<entity>/<action>/<target | sender>

   /sales/orders/create/salesforce
   /hr/customers/update/workday
   /finance/payments/authorize/stripe
   /iot/sensors/stream/mqtt
   /iot/devices/command/firmware

Here the domain is the primary routing key.

 Proxy Sprawl (1 proxy por backend or Contract)
   /sap-s4hana/orders/create
   /salesforce-crm/orders/create
   /oracle-erp/orders/create
   /legacy-system/orders/create
↑ 4 proxies · 4 contracts · 4 versioning

After with DEIP + SDIA + GDCR and DDCR

GDCR (1 proxy por domínio)

    /sales/<entity>/<action>/<vendor>

↑ 1 proxy · 1 contract · new vendor via metadata - KVM for SAP APIM

 

Implementation Note – PoC vs. Productive Landscapes
In all documents the /sales/<entity>/<action>/<target> pattern is used as a minimal PoC example to prove the DDCR invariant and the DEIP decision pipeline. This URL structure is valid for experimental validation, where a single GDCR proxy for /sales/ receives all Sales traffic. However, in a productive enterprise landscape this path should be extended with an explicit business subdomain to avoid over‑concentration of routes and to keep semantic separation between processes. A more robust pattern is:

/\<domain\>/\<subdomain\>/\<entity\>/\<action\>/\<target | sender\>

For example, within the SALES domain:

  • /sales/o2c/orders/create/salesforce – Order‑to‑Cash

  • /sales/l2o/leads/create/hubspot – Lead‑to‑Opportunity

  • /sales/q2o/quotes/approve/cpq – Quote‑to‑Order

In this model, you typically create one GDCR proxy per domain and subdomain, such as gdcr-proxy-domain-sales-o2c, with static routing on the /sales/o2c/<entity>/... segment, while DDCR remains responsible for resolving the final target via metadata. PoC examples using only /sales/ should therefore be interpreted as experimental simplifications, not as a recommended productive topology.

Entry door: https://<apim>/282cc9datrial/sales/ 

What does this configuration mean?

This is a static routing rule condition in the API Proxy design. This single proxy — SALES O2C — supports all calls from anywhere, covering 13 business processes from the O2C domain.

What does this still mean ?

500 applications · 1 proxy · 13 routing rules = End of proxy sprawl. The rest DDCR — JavaScript — resolves alone. Don't worry.

Why did I decide to segregate the proxy per business domain? To avoid the sprawl of a single proxy responsible for all calls. This segregation also keeps everything more organized.

Does DDCR work with OpenAPI and Swagger ?

Yes — the JavaScript is the routing engine. There is no connection with the contract or SLA of the specification. But the mindset must change: you don't create a specification per vendor — you create a specification per domain.

This means the specification, proxy, and DDCR JavaScript are immutable. If anything new comes to onboard — add one string in the KVM. That's it. As simple as 2+2=4. This is mathematics.

But my company is working with IOT etc... so follow the same process, create proxy, add the routing, what will be the domain /LLM/ thats it

gdcr-ai-llm-proxy-domain-unified

    https://<apim>/282cc9datrial/llm

SDIA → DDCR

Once a request enters the domain structure, DDCR (Domain Driven Centric Router) resolves the final integration configuration.

DDCR acts as a deterministic resolution engine.

It takes the resolved domain identity and looks up the metadata produced by the DEIP pipeline to determine:

  • protocol - http

  • endpoint - any destiny

Conceptually:

Domain + Intent → Configuration

So let's check again what was discussed above we are speaking about SALES - O2C means the entry door for SAP APIM:

Entry door: https://<apim>/282cc9datrial/sales/ 

Than we have this cenario - 1 proxy - routing ( /orders/** - / customers/** - /deliveries/** 😞

https://<sapapim>/sales/orders/create/salesforce
https://<sapapim>/sales/orders/update/salesforceemea
https://<sapapim>/sales/orders/create/microsoft
https://<sapapim>/sales/customers/sync/s4hana
https://<sapapim>/sales/customers/sync/shopify
https://<sapapim>/sales/deliveries/transfer/fedex

DDCR Keys — KVM (determinísticas e machine-computed)

dcrporderscid01salesforce:http
dcrporderscid02sfemea:http
dcrporderscid03microsoft:http
dcrpcustomerssid04s4hana:cxf
dcrpcustomerssid05shopify:http
dcrpdeliveriestid06fedex:http

Resolved URLs — CPI (execution-ready)

https://<cpi>/http/dcrp/orders/c/id01
https://<cpi>/http/dcrp/orders/u/id02
https://<cpi>/http/dcrp/orders/c/id03
https://<cpi>/cxf/dcrp/customers/s/id04
https://<cpi>/http/dcrp/customers/s/id05
https://<cpi>/http/dcrp/deliveries/t/id06

Again — this logic is pure mathematics. The only way to fail is a wrong intent HTTP path or a wrong string in the KVM, that triggers in the 3rd step the FAST-FAIL M2M in ~0.1ms, the backend don't even know about this call. It never fails. Over 2 million calls across 5 different API platforms from the market — and everything else I tested — zero routing failures.

SDIA → ODCP

Some integrations require orchestration across multiple domains. This is where ODCP — Orchestration Domain-Centric Pattern — operates.

ODCP governs three things simultaneously:

Package Topology — One Domain, One Package

Before:                          After:
Salesforce_Orders_Pkg        →   nx.sales.o2c.integrations
SAP_S4HANA_Orders_Pkg        →   (same package — Sales domain)
SAP_ECC_FI_AR_v2_Package     →   nx.finance.r2r.integrations
FedEx_Shipping_Integration   →   nx.logistics.le.integrations
Ariba_Procurement_Pkg        →   nx.procurement.s2p.integrations
Temp_QA_DoNotDelete          →   eliminated

39 packages → 4. Package count becomes invariant — it scales with domains, not vendors.

ODCP defines a structured package naming convention that encodes domain identity, business process, and platform context into a single readable string:

Component

Description

Example Values

Constraints

prefix

Organization or platform identifier

nx, sap, az, aws, mule

Lowercase, alphanumeric

domain

Business domain

sales, finance, logistics, procurement, hr, manufacturing

Lowercase, no spaces

process

Primary business process subdomain

o2c, r2r, le, s2p, h2r, p2p

Standard process codes

integrations

Fixed suffix

integrations

Always literal 'integrations'

[prefix].[domain].[process].integrations  Multi-division — same domain, same business process, one division owns the integration:

[prefix-company].[division].[domain].[businessprocess].[integration]

Multi-division — same orchestration tenant, multiple divisions, same domain:

[prefix-company].[division].[core].[domain]

iFlow DNA — Deterministic Artifact Naming

POST /sales/orders/create/salesforce
→ id01.o2c.salesforce.order.s4hana.c.in.sync

POST /finance/invoices/create/s4hana
→ id13.r2r.s4hana.invoice.target.c.in.sync

Every artifact is traceable from semantic URL → DDCR key → iFlow DNA. No more Salesforce_Order_Sync_v3_FINAL_USE_THIS.

id[seq].[subdomain].[sender].[entity].[receiver].[action].[direction].[sync|async]

Example:  id01.o2c.salesforce.order.s4hana.c.in.sync

iFlow DNA Component Reference

 

Component

Description

Example Values

Constraints

Role

id[seq]

Sequential flow identifier within the package

id01, id02, id99

Pattern id\d{2}

Links to DDCR KVM iflowid

subdomain

Business process code

o2c, r2r, le, s2p

Standard process codes

Process context

sender

Source system or vendor

salesforce, sap, workday, shopify

Lowercase, alphanumeric

Integration source

entity

Business entity being processed

order, invoice, customer, shipment

Lowercase, singular

Business object

receiver

Target backend system

s4hana, oracle, stripe, target

Lowercase, alphanumeric

Integration target

action

Canonical action code

c, r, u, d, s, n, a, t

Single character — DDCR codes

Operation type

direction

Message flow direction

in, out

Lowercase

Flow direction

sync|async

Execution mode

sync, async

Lowercase

Processing mode

 

iFlow DNA — Implementation Proofs

 

Semantic Address

iFlow DNA

POST /sales/orders/create/salesforce

id01.o2c.salesforce.order.s4hana.c.in.sync

GET /finance/invoices/get/s4hana

id10.r2r.s4hana.invoice.target.r.in.sync

POST /finance/invoices/create/s4hana

id13.r2r.s4hana.invoice.target.c.in.sync

PUT /sales/customers/update/salesforceus

id02.o2c.salesforce.customer.s4hana.u.in.sync

POST /logistics/shipments/sync/microsoft

id21.le.microsoft.shipment.s4hana.s.in.async

POST /procurement/requisitions/create/ariba

id31.s2p.ariba.requisition.s4hana.c.in.sync

PUT /procurement/contracts/update/workday

id35.s2p.workday.contract.s4hana.u.in.sync

POST /finance/journals/post/s4hana

id14.r2r.s4hana.journal.target.a.in.sync

Each iFlow DNA name is directly traceable to its semantic address in the GDCR facade and its KVM routing key in the DDCR engine. The id[seq] component is the explicit link: id01 in the iFlow DNA corresponds to iflowid id01 in the KVM key dcrporderscsalesforceid01:http.

The Semantic Chain — End-to-End Traceability

 

Semantic Address

DDCR KVM Key

DDCR Resolved Path

ODCP iFlow DNA

POST /sales/orders/create/salesforce

dcrporderscsalesforceid01:http

/http/dcrp/orders/c/salesforce/id01

id01.o2c.salesforce.order.s4hana.c.in.sync

POST /finance/invoices/create/s4hana

dcrpinvoicescs4hanaid13:cxf

/cxf/dcrp/invoices/c/s4hana/id13

id13.r2r.s4hana.invoice.target.c.in.sync

PUT /sales/customers/update/salesforceus

dcrpcustomersu salesforceusid02:http

/http/dcrp/customers/u/salesforceus/id02

id02.o2c.salesforce.customer.s4hana.u.in.sync

POST /procurement/requisitions/create/ariba

dcrprequisitions cariba id31:cxf

/cxf/dcrp/requisitions/c/ariba/id31

id31.s2p.ariba.requisition.s4hana.c.in.sync

Business intent enters at the semantic address. Metadata decides at the DDCR engine. Domain governs at the ODCP orchestration layer. Technology becomes background. Semantics become the integration language — end to end.

Credential Segmentation — Sub-Domain Tiered

cr.sales.orders.rw      — Orders only · 30 days rotation
cr.sales.analytics.ro   — Analytics only · 90 days rotation
cr.finance.ap.rw        — Accounts Payable only · 30 days rotation

39 credentials → 12. One rotation failure affects only one sub-domain — never the entire landscape.

The result: Business intent enters at the semantic URL. DDCR resolves. ODCP governs the artifact. Technology becomes background.

Credential Naming Convention

Technical user names follow the same domain‑centric semantic pattern as packages and flows:

Examples:

  • cr.sales.orders.rw – Sales / Orders / Read‑Write
  • cr.finance.ap.rw – Finance / Accounts Payable / Read‑Write
  • cr.finance.reporting.ro – Finance / Reporting / Read‑Only
  • cr.logistics.shipments.rw – Logistics / Shipments / Read‑Write

The naming convention makes credential ownership, scope, and permission level immediately readable from the identifier, eliminating the opacity of names like iflow_cred_12 or api_user_SF_orders.

Three Pillars of ODCP Credential Governance

 

Pillar

Principle

Outcome

Consolidation

Group flows by domain sub-domain, not by project or vendor

Eliminates credential silos and naming chaos

Standardization

Enforce consistent naming: prefix.domain.subdomain.permission

Enables systematic credential audit and rotation

Governance

Assign ownership by sub-domain architect; enforce RBAC on credential mutation

Centralized control, isolated blast radius, audit-ready

 

SAP-Specific Artifact Scope

In SAP BTP Cloud Integration, the iFlow DNA naming convention extends to all integration artifacts within the package — not only iFlows. Script collections, value mappings, message mappings, data stores, communication channels, technical users, and even internal variables adopt the same domain‑centric prefixing (for example ,vm.sales.order.status, sc.sales.order.transformation, cr.sales.orders.rw). This makes the entire CPI artifact space navigable by business domain rather than by technical type or vendor.

Artifact Type

Naming Convention

Integration Flows (iFlows)

id[seq].[subdomain].[sender].[entity].[receiver].[action].[direction].[mode]

Value Mapping Tables

vm.[domain].[entity].[field] — e.g. vm.sales.order.status

Script Collections

sc.[domain].[function] — e.g. sc.sales.order.transformation

Message Mappings

mm.[domain].[entity].[sender].[receiver] — e.g. mm.sales.order.sfdc.s4h

Technical Users

cr.[domain].[subdomain].[permission] — e.g. cr.sales.orders.rw

Internal Variables

var.[domain].[context].[name] — e.g. var.sales.orders.batchsize

 

SDIA — COMPLETE ARCHITECTURE

End-to-End Semantic Flow

The Semantic Domain Integration Architecture (SDIA) preserves business domain semantics across every layer of the enterprise integration stack. ODCP is the final governance layer in this chain — ensuring that semantic continuity extends from the API consumer address to the backend execution artifact.

Human Semantic Address

POST /sales/orders/create/salesforce

GDCR

Gateway Layer

Semantic gateway facade  —  domain-centric API governance

Single proxy per domain  ·  semantic URL abstraction  ·  fail-fast enforcement

DDCR

Resolution Engine

Deterministic 7-stage runtime resolution  —  metadata-driven backend binding

dcrporderscsalesforceid01:http  →  /http/dcrp/orders/c/salesforce/id01

ODCP

Orchestration Layer

Domain-centric package topology  —  iFlow DNA  —  tiered credential governance

nx.sales.o2c.integrations  ·  id01.o2c.salesforce.order.s4hana.c.in.sync

Enterprise Systems

SAP S/4HANA  ·  Salesforce  ·  Oracle  ·  Workday  ·  Stripe  ·  Legacy Systems  ·  Cloud Platforms

The Result

When combined, these layers create a complete architecture:

DEIP    — Semantic Control Plane      → What · Why · Which
   ↓
SDIA    — Domain Runtime Architecture → Organized by domain intent
   ↓
GDCR    — Gateway Layer               → 1 proxy per domain · immutable
   ↓
DDCR    — Resolution Engine           → f(key) → value · zero failures
   ↓
ODCP    — Orchestration Layer         → 1 package · DNA naming · tiered credentials
   ↓
Execution Platforms                   → SAP · AWS · Azure · Kong · IoT · AI/LLM
   ↓
Result  = Sales + Sales + Sales + Sales

The full end-to-end — based on the intention of the Sales domain. The technology does not matter. What matters is what the domain needs.

Conclusion

Most enterprise integration landscapes converge to a small set of recurring failure modes. Not because of bad engineers. Not because of bad platforms. Because the organizing principle was always wrong.

Technology was never the primary key. The domain always was.

DEIP does not add a new tool to your stack. It changes the question you ask before you build anything:

What is the domain intent of this integration?

Answer that first. Everything else — protocol, platform, proxy, credential, artifact — becomes a consequence.

The domain never lies. It never has. We just forgot to ask it first.

Start Here — Reading Order

⚠️Before reading anything else — start with GDCR.

GDCR is where everything began. Originally developed as DCRP and PDCP — two SAP-specific patterns — it evolved into the foundation of the entire SDIA ecosystem. Without reading GDCR first, the other documents will not make sense. For full understanding, the recommended starting point is GDCR.

Reading order:

  1. 🔗GDCR — DOI: 10.5281/zenodo.18582492 ← Start here
  2. 🔗DDCR — DOI: 10.5281/zenodo.18864832
  3. 🔗ODCP — DOI: 10.5281/zenodo.18876594
  4. 🔗SDIA — DOI: 10.5281/zenodo.18877635
  5. 🔗DEIP — DOI: 10.5281/zenodo.19004802 ← This document

What comes next

In approximately two months I will release a companion document covering how to build the complete DEIP integration map and navigate all four architectural layers in practice. I cannot commit to an exact date yet.

After that — my focus moves to IEEE. The mathematics behind DDCR and DEIP deserve a formal academic submission. That is where this work goes next.

The domain never lies.

Ricardo Luz Holanda Viana — March 2026 — Warsaw, Poland

entry.title: Bulk Send Documents to Multiple Signers using Docusign Adapter
entry.content:
The Docusign Adapter for SAP Integration Suite, simplifies and accelerates integration while ensuring secure authentication and connectivity with DocuSign. It enables seamless embedding of e-signature capabilities into your workflows. The adapter streamlines processes such as contract signing, purchase order approvals, and other document-based transactions, resulting in faster processing, enhanced compliance, and a smoother user experience.

Bulk Send using Docusign Adapter

In this scenario, we will demonstrate how to create a template (containing a signable pdf), and send it to multiple signers. We will add the signer's email to the bulk send list used for the bulk send request.

Creating Credentials to connect to Docusign

We will use OAuth 2.0 Authorization Code for this scenario.

OAuth Authentication: The OAuth 2.0 Authorization Code flow is a secure method for applications, especially server-based ones, to access a user’s data without directly handling their credentials.  For more info, see section 3.3 in the Adapter Guide.

To create the Oauth2 Authorization Code in your SAP environment, specify the artifact Name, Authorization URL, Token Service URL, and the Client ID (Integration Key) from your DocuSign account. Refer figure below to configure the settings.

Iflow Design in SAP CI 

The figure below demonstrates provides a representation of how the adapter works.

Let us break down the three parts to this scenario. 

  • We will create a template in Docusign which has a pdf (encoded as base64); the template must have the signer roles added to it. We will add three roles since we want it to be sent to 3 approvers. You can modify this part to add more.
  • Secondly, we create a bulk send list , which contains the Names and emails of the recepients. Note that the number of signers in the template must match those in the bulk send list.
  • Thirdly, we  use the templateID for step#1 and listId from step#2 to send a bulk request for signature.

Connect to Docusign via Docusign Adapter

Now you must configure the Connection tab of the adapter, specify Address and select Authentication as OAuth 2.0 Authorization Code. Specify the Authorization Code Credentials that was created previously in SAP Security Material. For more information about Connection tab parameters, see Connection in SAP Help.

Convert your PDF to Base64

Create a PDF and convert it into base64; the signer tab position can be set in the payload.

Operation 1:  Create a Template in DocuSign (with a PDF)

In the Content Modifier, you can define the name, description and signer fields.

{
  "name": "Student Activities Form",
  "description": "Form for bulk signing with anchor-based signature fields",
  "emailSubject": "Student Activities Form",
  "emailBlurb": "Dear Student, please enter the details and sign",
  "status": "created",
  "documents": [
    {
      "documentId": "1",
      "name": "Student_Form.pdf",
      "fileExtension": "pdf",
      "documentBase64": "<Your base 64 encoded content goes here>"
    }
  ],
  "recipients": {
    "signers": [
      {
        "roleName": "Signer 1",
        "recipientId": "1",
        "routingOrder": "1",
        "tabs": {
          "signHereTabs": [
            {
              "documentId": "1",
              "tabLabel": "SignatureField",
              "anchorString": "/SignHere/",
              "anchorXOffset": "4.3",
              "anchorYOffset": "10.5",
              "anchorUnits": "cms",
              "anchorIgnoreIfNotPresent": "false"
            }
          ],
          "dateSignedTabs": [
            {
              "documentId": "1",
              "tabLabel": "DateSigned",
              "anchorString": "/DateSigned/",
              "anchorXOffset": "4.3",
              "anchorYOffset": "11.5",
              "anchorUnits": "cms",
              "anchorIgnoreIfNotPresent": "false"
            }
          ],
          "fullNameTabs": [
            {
              "documentId": "1",
              "tabLabel": "FullName",
              "anchorString": "/FullName/",
              "anchorXOffset": "4.3",
              "anchorYOffset": "12.5",
              "anchorUnits": "cms",
              "anchorIgnoreIfNotPresent": "false"
            }
          ],
          "titleTabs": [
            {
              "documentId": "1",
              "tabLabel": "Title",
              "anchorString": "/Title/",
              "anchorXOffset": "4.3",
              "anchorYOffset": "13.5",
              "anchorUnits": "cms",
              "anchorIgnoreIfNotPresent": "false"
            }
          ],
          "textTabs": [
            {
              "documentId": "1",
              "tabLabel": "Name",
              "anchorString": "/Name/",
              "anchorXOffset": "4.3",
              "anchorYOffset": "14.5",
              "anchorUnits": "cms",
              "anchorIgnoreIfNotPresent": "false",
              "required": "false",
              "locked": "false",
              "width": "150",
              "height": "20"
            },
            {
              "documentId": "1",
              "tabLabel": "Value",
              "anchorString": "/Value/",
              "anchorXOffset": "5.3",
              "anchorYOffset": "15.5",
              "anchorUnits": "cms",
              "anchorIgnoreIfNotPresent": "false",
              "required": "false",
              "locked": "false",
              "width": "150",
              "height": "20"
            }
          ]
        }
      }
    ]
  },
  "notification": {
    "useAccountDefaults": "false",
    "reminders": {
      "reminderEnabled": "true",
      "reminderDelay": "2",
      "reminderFrequency": "3"
    },
    "expirations": {
      "expireEnabled": "true",
      "expireAfter": "30",
      "expireWarn": "5"
    }
  }
}

Response: Once the template is created, the response will contain the TemplateId. Store this TemplateId in a header for use in subsequent steps.

Navigate to the next request reply pallet and in processing, select the "Create one or more templates" operation.

Operation 2: Create a Bulk Send List with Recipient Emails

Now, let create a list of receipts to create a bulk send list. In the Content Modifier, you can define and configure the recipient details, such as names, email addresses, or identifiers, to ensure the document is sent to the intended parties accurately and efficiently.

{
  "name": "Student Bulk List - Activities",
  "bulkCopies": [
    {
      "recipients": [
        {
          "roleName": "Signer 1",
          "name": "Signer1",
          "email": "Singer1@example.com"
        }
      ]
    },
    {
      "recipients": [
        {
          "roleName": "Signer 1",
          "name": "Signer2",
          "email": "Singer2@example.com"
        }
      ]
    },
    {
      "recipients": [
        {
          "roleName": "Signer 1",
          "name": "Signer3",
          "email": "Singer3@example.com"
        }
      ]
    }
  ]
}

Response: Once the Send List is created, the response will contain the BulkSendListId. Store this ListId in a header for use in subsequent steps.

Navigate to the next request reply pallet and in processing, select the "Create bulk send list" operation.

Operation 3:  Send Template to Multiple Signers/Recipients

Now let us send our document to multiple users.

In a Content Modifier, you need to use the TemplateId that was previously stored in the header.

{
  "envelopeOrTemplateId": "${header.templateID}",
  "mailingListId": "${header.ListId}",
  "senderDisplaySettings": {
    "senderName": "<SenderName>",
    "senderEmailAddress": "<SenderEmail>"
  }
}

Navigate to the next request reply pallet and in processing, select the "Create bulk send request" operation.

Response: Once the Send Request is created, we will receive the requestId to track further.

Result

Once the above process is completed, the signers will receive an email to sign the document.

Click Continue.

Once signed, click Finish. 

Final Thoughts

This Iflow can be modified for scenarios where you want to send documents for a larger list of recipients. For more information, see the Docusign Adapter for SAP Integration Suite.

entry.title: Mapping SAP Process Orchestration JMS (IBM MQ) Adapter to SAP Integration Suite IBM MQ Adapter
entry.content:
This blog post is part of the series Master Blog: SAP Process Orchestration to SAP Integration Suite Migration – Tool and Guidance exploring how to migrate various integration patterns from SAP Process Orchestration to SAP Integration Suite using the Migration Tool.

Objective of this Blog

In this blog, we will focus on the mapping of SAP Process Orchestration JMS (IBM MQ) adapter — with Transport Protocol “WebSphereMQ (MQSeries) JMS Provider” and Message Protocol both “JMS 1.x” or “JMS 2.0”) — to SAP Integration Suite IBM MQ adapter.

Since the Migration Tool skips the SAP Process Orchestration JMS (IBM MQ) adapter, the approach described in this blog will help to manually configure both Sender and Receiver IBM MQ adapters in SAP Integration Suite iFlows based on source JMS (IBM MQ) SAP Process Orchestration scenarios.

Related Blogs 

  • IBM MQ JMS Adapter for SAP Integration Suite
  • Integrating IBM MQ On-Premise with SAP Cloud Integration Using the IBM MQ Adapter

SAP Process Orchestration JMS Adapter Modules

In SAP Process Orchestration, the following JMS Adapter Modules are commonly used to convert message payloads between binary and XML formats:                       

  • SAP XI JMS Adapter/ConvertJMSMessageToBinary
  • SAP XI JMS Adapter/ConvertBinaryToXMBMessage
  • SAP XI JMS Adapter/ConvertMessageToBinary
  • SAP XI JMS Adapter/SendBinarytoXIJMSService

In SAP Process Orchestration, these modules are often required when integrating with JMS providers such as IBM MQ. However, in SAP Integration Suite they are not required, as their functionality is provided directly by the IBM MQ adapter itself. The IBM MQ adapter in SAP Integration Suite offers built-in options for message conversion, allowing users to select either byte or text message formats directly in the adapter configuration.

Variants

Based on the SAP Process Orchestration source parameters, the migration will result in three different target variants:

- Sender

  • JMS Poll
  • JMS Subscribe

- Receiver:

  • JMS

Sender

The source Sender JMS MQ channel must have the following properties:

  • Adapter Type: JMS
  • Sender
  • Transport Protocol: WebSphereMQ (MQSeries) JMS Provider
  • Message protocol: JMS 1.x or JMS 2.0

The relevant Sender variants — JMS Poll and JMS Subscribe — are detailed below (REST variant doesn’t apply).

JMS Poll

JMS Poll implements a message retrieval mechanism where the IBM MQ JMS Sender Adapter actively polls messages from IBM MQ queues or topics at defined intervals, using a request-based approach to check for new messages periodically.

Condition

In SAP Process Orchestration, for this variant to apply, the following condition must be met:

“Use Message Listener Based Connector” checkbox in the Processing view not checked:

Mapping (SAP Process Orchestration → SAP Integration Suite)

The following table shows the mapping from SAP Process Orchestration to SAP Integration Suite:

 

SAP Process Orchestration

SAP Process Orchestration

SAP Integration Suite

SAP Integration Suite

#

View

Parameter

View

Parameter

1

2

3

Connection

4

5

Processing

Message Destination Type = Topic → Topic Name/String

6

7

9

10

Processing

Source

Use SSL checked and WSMQ Cipher Spec = Other → Other Cipher Suite

SAP Process Orchestration

The following SAP Process Orchestration screenshots illustrate the mapping:

Source view

If “Use SSL” checked and “WSMQ Cipher Spec” = “WebSphereMQ Cipher Spec”:

If “Use SSL” checked and “WSMQ Cipher Spec” = “Other”:

Processing view

SAP Integration Suite

The following SAP Integration Suite screenshots illustrate the mapping:

Connection view

* After maintaining the Credentials in the SAP Integration Suite Security Materials section, the alias name must be provided in the Credential Name field to establish the connection.

Processing view

If “Message Destination” = “Topic”:

If “Message Destination” = “Queue”:

 JMS Subscribe

JMS Subscribe implements a message retrieval mechanism where the IBM MQ JMS Sender Adapter establishes an active subscription to IBM MQ queues or topics, enabling real-time or near real-time message delivery as soon as messages are published to the destination.

Condition

In SAP Process Orchestration, for this variant to apply, the following condition must be met:

“Use Message Listener Based Connector” checkbox in the Processing view checked:

Mapping (SAP Process Orchestration → SAP Integration Suite)

Same mapping as for the JMS Poll variant, but without fields 14, 15, and 16.

Receiver

The source Receiver JMS MQ channel must have the following properties: 

  • Adapter Type: JMS
  • Receiver
  • Transport Protocol: WebSphereMQ (MQSeries) JMS Provider
  • Message protocol: JMS 1.x or JMS 2.0

The relevant Receiver variant — JMS — is detailed below (HTTPS variant doesn’t apply):

JMS

JMS Receiver implements a message publishing mechanism where the IBM MQ JMS Receiver Adapter enables SAP Integration Suite to send messages to IBM MQ queues or topics, acting as a message producer that publishes data to designated destinations in the IBM MQ server.

Mapping (SAP Process Orchestration → SAP Integration Suite)

The following table shows the mapping from SAP Process Orchestration to SAP Integration Suite:

 

SAP Process Orchestration

SAP Process Orchestration

The following SAP Process Orchestration screenshots illustrate the mapping:

Target view

If “Use SSL” checked and “WSMQ Cipher Spec” = “WebSphereMQ Cipher Spec”:

SAP Integration Suite

The following SAP Integration Suite screenshots illustrate the mapping:

Connection view

*After maintaining the Credentials in the SAP Integration Suite Security Materials section, the alias name must be provided in the Credential Name field to establish the connection.

Processing view

If “Message Destination” = “Topic”:

If “Message Destination” = “Queue”:

Advanced view

Conclusion

By following the mapping approach outlined in this blog — including detailed mapping tables and screenshots for the different target SAP Integration Suite IBM MQ sender and receiver adapter variants — users can successfully migrate their adapters from SAP Process Orchestration scenarios to SAP Integration Suite iFlows.

 * Since not all parameters can be mapped, end users must evaluate whether any functionality configured in the source SAP Process Orchestration JMS (IBM MQ) adapter has not been migrated to the target SAP Integration Suite IBM MQ adapter (e.g., PI Settings, Error-Handling Settings, or Duplicate Handling).

** In addition, some functionalities — such as correlation ID mapping — cannot be mapped directly but can be configured in the SAP Integration Suite IBM MQ adapter. E.g., in the receiver adapter, it can be set dynamically using an exchange header or property.

entry.title: Upload binary content via Cloud Integration to SAP with BDS_BUSINESSDOCUMENT_CREA_TAB
entry.content:
SAP Generic Object Services (GOS) is commonly used to store attachments for business objects such as sales orders, purchase orders, or deliveries.

In SAP PI/PO, many implementations use the RFC function module BDS_BUSINESSDOCUMENT_CREA_TAB to upload binary content to SAP. However, when working with SAP Cloud Integration, handling binary content requires a slightly different approach.

In this blog post, I will demonstrate how to build a simple iFlow that uploads a file from an external system to SAP and stores it as a GOS attachment using the RFC function module BDS_BUSINESSDOCUMENT_CREA_TAB.

Scenario Overview

The scenario works as follows:

  • A file is uploaded to an SFTP server.
  • Cloud Integration processes the file and converts it into the format required by BDS_BUSINESSDOCUMENT_CREA_TAB.
  • The RFC adapter calls the SAP backend system via Cloud Connector.
  • The document is stored in SAP and can be accessed via transaction OAOR.

Below is the overall iFlow design.

Processing Steps

Step 1 – Base64 Encode the File

The file content is first Base64 encoded. This prevents issues caused by special characters that may interfere with XML processing in later steps.

Step 2 – Prepare the XML Structure

Next, we prepare an XML message that will be used as the input for message mapping. 

Example structure:

<MT_File>
	<FileName>${header.CamelFileName}</FileName>
	<FileSize>${header.CamelFileLength}</FileSize>
	<Content>${body}</Content>
</MT_File>

Step 3 – Convert XML into RFC Request

The XML message is then mapped into the RFC structure required by the function module BDS_BUSINESSDOCUMENT_CREA_TAB. Example request:

<?xml version="1.0" encoding="UTF-8"?>
<ns1:BDS_BUSINESSDOCUMENT_CREA_TAB xmlns:ns1="urn:sap-com:document:sap:rfc:functions">
	<OBJECT_KEY>0000000001</OBJECT_KEY>
	<BINARY_FLAG>X</BINARY_FLAG>
	<CLASSNAME>SOFFICEINTEGRATION</CLASSNAME>
	<CLASSTYPE>OT</CLASSTYPE>
	<COMPONENTS>
		<item>
			<DOC_COUNT>00000000</DOC_COUNT>
			<MIMETYPE>text/plain</MIMETYPE>
			<COMP_SIZE>1957</COMP_SIZE>
		</item>
	</COMPONENTS>
	<CONTENT>
		<item>
			<LINE>446965204DC3BC...E62726F74</LINE>
		</item>
		<item>
			<LINE>656E20756E...CC39FC3A4</LINE>
		</item>
	</CONTENT>
	<SIGNATURE>
		<item>
			<DOC_COUNT>00000000</DOC_COUNT>
			<PROP_NAME>BDS_DOCUMENTCLASS</PROP_NAME>
			<PROP_VALUE>TXT</PROP_VALUE>
		</item>
		<item>
			<DOC_COUNT>00000000</DOC_COUNT>
			<PROP_NAME>BDS_DOCUMENTTYPE</PROP_NAME>
			<PROP_VALUE>TXT</PROP_VALUE>
		</item>
		<item>
			<DOC_COUNT>00000000</DOC_COUNT>
			<PROP_NAME>DESCRIPTION</PROP_NAME>
			<PROP_VALUE>german_text.txt</PROP_VALUE>
		</item>
	</SIGNATURE>
</ns1:BDS_BUSINESSDOCUMENT_CREA_TAB>

Important Parameters 

OBJECT_KEY: The identifier of the business object. For example, for a purchase order it would be like 4500000001, for delivery it would be like 0034423362. 

BINARY_FLAG: Set to X because the request contains binary data.

CLASSNAME: Defines the GOS class for the object. For example, `EKKO` would be for pruchase order, `LIKP` would be for delivery. 

CLASSTYPE: 'BO' for business object, 'OT' for others
