After participating in the SAP Multidimensional Architect Program at the SAP Academy  in California during Fall 2025, I was particularly inspired by a session titled “From Blueprint to Real Architecture,” which showed how to design end-to-end solutions, one of the cases presented, was international trade scenario, area of my personal interest over the last few years. That experience motivated me to write this Proof of Concept.

This article focuses on the scenario of Ocean Carriers transitioning from paper-based processes to a fully digital platform, aiming to reduce CO₂ emissions while ensuring security and transparency among stakeholders. The solution implements the Electronic Bill of Lading using IMDA's  TradeTrust , an open-source framework that enables the creation of digital trade documents represented as NFT(ERC-721 tokens) on a blockchain network.

This PoC demonstrates how this capability can be deployed on SAP Business Technology Platform, aligned with Digital Container Shipping Association (DCSA)  standards.

 

Article Summary

This three-part series demonstrates how international trade can move from paper-based operations to secure and automate processes while helping organizations achieve their sustainability goals

  • Part 1 – Case Study: Introduces the shift in global trade from paper-based documents to Electronic Bills of Lading.
  • Part 2 – Blueprint:  Describes the key building blocks of the solution, including: event-driven architecture, SAP BTP, Automation, TradeTrust Framework, UI, and the architecture diagram.
  • Part 3 – End-to-End Scenario: Shows the lifecycle of the Electronic Bill of Lading through a real-world scenario.

 

1. Case Study

The global shipping industry, responsible for approximately 90% of world trade, faces pressure to reduce its environmental footprint. Traditional paper-based Bills of Lading, critical for international trade, contribute to deforestation, water pollution, and greenhouse gas emissions through their production, transportation, and disposal.

2. Business Context

Paper Bills of Lading(BL) plays a crucial role in international trade, serve as Receipt, Contract and Document of title. This document is been the core of international trade for centuries.

  • Receipt: The BL, which is issued by the carrier, serves as proof that the cargo has been loaded onto the vessel, documenting the quantity and condition of the goods at the time of shipment.
  • Contract: It acts as a contract of carriage between the shipper, carrier and consignee, defining the agreed terms and conditions of transportation.
  • Document of title: It acts as a document of title, representing ownership of the goods while they are in transit. Possession of the original bill of lading allows the holder to claim the goods upon their arrival or arrange transfer ownership of the cargo to another party in the supply chain.

 

The process typically starts with the carrier issuing the BL to the shipper/seller upon receiving the goods. The shipper/seller then sends the original bill of lading to the buyer, often through a bank, to ensure that the seller receives the payment before the goods are released to the buyer. Finally, the consignee/buyer presents the original bill of lading to the carrier at the destination port to take possession of the goods.

Problem

Traditionally, this has been paper-based, the document must travel physically between banks and buyers across countries, requiring couriers, manual signatures, and physical handovers. Each party checks it manually, which takes 5-10 days, and if the cargo is traded while the ship is at sea, the bill changes hands multiple times. This often causes ships to wait at port, creates demurrage costs, and forces parties to use risky workarounds like letters of indemnity, which carry legal and financial risks.

                                                                 Total time needed for B/L transfer via express courier service: 5-10 days

Solution

The emergence of blockchain technology has enabled the development of Electronic Bills of Lading(eBL), offering not only enhanced security and efficiency but also the potential for a substantial reduction in paper consumption and associated carbon emissions.

Blockchain provides a decentralized and transparent system to manage eBL. The data is not controlled by a single party, but shared across a network where all participants can verify the transactions. Every action, such as issuing or transferring the eBL, is recorded on the blockchain and can be traced by authorized parties. Transactions are validated almost in real time, allowing the eBL to be transferred within minutes instead of several days required with paper documents sent by courier.

By eliminating millions of paper documents and the need for courier shipments, organizations can reduce delays, lower operational costs, minimize the risk of errors, and decrease carbon footprints while aligning with ESG commitments. Electronic bills of lading on an Ethereum Layer 2 can cut carbon emissions by ~96% compared to traditional paper B/Ls.

Electronic Bill of Lading Lifecycle[4]

 

The benefits of the eBL are many and huge. In the announcement, DCSA wrote: “Switching away from the transfer of physical paper bills of lading could save $6.5 billion in direct costs for stakeholders, enable $30-40 billion in annual global trade growth, transform the customer experience and improve sustainability.”

Payment Settlement in International Trade

In traditional international trade, payments are typically secured through banking instruments such as Letters of Credit, documentary collections, or bank guarantees. In these models, banks act as trusted intermediaries that verify trade documents, including the Bill of Lading, before releasing payment from the importer to the exporter. While these mechanisms provide security, they are often manual, time-consuming, and highly dependent on paper-based documentation.

As trade processes move toward digital documentation, new opportunities emerge to integrate payment mechanisms directly with Electronic Bills of Lading.

By combining digital payments with eBL, parts of the trade process could be automated. For example, a smart contract on a blockchain funded by the importer could hold the payment until predefined conditions are met. Once ownership of the eBL is transferred to the importer, the smart contract could automatically release the funds to the exporter. In this context, stablecoins may play an important role by enabling fast and programmable digital settlement.

At the same time, modern banks increasingly provide APIs for Letter of Credit verification and trade finance services. Through networks such as SWIFT, financial institutions can exchange standardized payment messages and verification data. These capabilities can be integrated into digital trade platforms, helping automate document verification.

Although payment is not explored in detail within the scope of this Proof of Concept, the architecture presented here is designed to support such capabilities in the future, allowing payment orchestration to be incorporated as the trade ecosystem continues to evolve.

The Future of Global Trade

In February 2023, DCSA announced a multi-carrier agreement to get to 100% usage of electronic Bills of Lading by 2030.

Thomas Bagge, chief executive officer, DCSA stated, “The digitalization of international trade holds vast potential for the world economy by reducing friction and, as trade brings prosperity and the eBL will further enable trade, helping bring millions out of poverty. Document digitalization has the power to transform and requires collaboration from all stakeholders.”

3. Solution Implementation

In order to develop a fully digitalized solution for international trade that leverages blockchain technology for the Electronic Bill of Lading, the solution should be aligned with industry standards to ensure interoperability, operational efficiency, and regulatory compliance. The solution follows the guidelines established by the Digital Container Shipping Association (DCSA)  and the United Nations Centre for Trade Facilitation and Electronic Business(UN/CEFACT) .

To support the implementation of the Electronic Bill of Lading, this Proof of Concept leverages TradeTrust , an open-source framework developed by the Infocomm Media Development Authority (IMDA) . TradeTrust provides a neutral infrastructure for the digitization and secure exchange of trade documents, aiming to make international trade safer, faster, easier, and more cost-effective.

The framework uses a public blockchain to maintain a single source-of-truth record of document ownership and aligns with the principles of the MLETR(Model Law on Electronic Transferable Records) , established by the UNCITRAL(United Nations Commission on International Trade Law). 

How TradeTrust Works

Conclusion

In Part 1, I explored the benefits of the Electronic Bill of Lading and its potential to transform the industry—driving sustainability, improving transparency across the supply chain, and accelerating global trade operations.

🔗In Part 2 , I’ll walk through the technical design and core building blocks behind the solution.

entry.title: Time zone conversion for message mapping in Integration Suite
entry.content:
Time zone conversion is not often used in Integration Suite, as date and time are mostly converted to different time zone in target system for most integrations. More recently however, with more standardized and cloud systems being used (e.g S4HANA Cloud), as well as prepackaged API's, time zone conversion has become more common. In addition, most countries use daylight savings time, which requires a different time offset depending on the day and month for date and time.

For SAP PO, the most common way to convert date and time to a different timezone is to use java libraries for Joda-Time in a user defined function to convert date and time to a different timezone. For Integration Suite, these libraries can be used by default in groovyscript. Following an earlier post with an example of how to use these libraries in groovy, as well as some changes to the code to make the function more generic, the code can be used in message mapping to convert date and time to a different timezone.

import java.util.TimeZone;
import org.joda.time.DateTime;
import org.joda.time.DateTimeZone;
import org.joda.time.format.DateTimeFormat;
import org.joda.time.format.DateTimeFormatter;

def String converttimezone(String input, String pattern, String totimezone) {
    
    //set output to empty
    def output = "";

    //if input is not empty, convert input to timezone
    if (input != "") {
        DateTimeFormatter dtformatter = DateTimeFormat.forPattern(pattern);
        DateTime datetime = dtformatter.parseDateTime(input);
        TimeZone timezone = TimeZone.getTimeZone(totimezone);
        output = datetime.withZone(DateTimeZone.forTimeZone(timezone)).toString();
    }
    
    return output;
}

The function uses parameters input, pattern and totimezone to convert a date and time to a different timezone

  • input: date and time with offset or timezone (e.g 2026-03-26T18:00:00+01:00)
  • pattern: format for date and time, using notation for SimpleDateFormat

    Most commonly used notation:

    • y - year
    • M - month
    • d - day
    • H - hour
    • m - minutes
    • s - seconds
    • S - milliseconds
    • Z - time offset (e.g +01:00, +0100)
    • ZZZ - time zone (e.g UTC, Europe/Amsterdam); this can be any identifier for time zone
      • Any text which is not part of the date and time notation can be enclosed in apostrophe (e.g yyyy-MM-dd'T'HH:mm:ss)
  • totimezone: any identifier for time zone (e.g UTC, Europe/Amsterdam)

The output date and time format for the function is always the same, either yyyy-MM-dd'T'HH:mm:ss.SSS'Z' (e.g 2026-03-26T18:00:00Z) when converting date and time to UTC, or yyyy-MM-dd'T'HH:mm:ss.SSSZ (e.g 2026-03-26T18:00:00+01:00) for any other time zone. If needed, the standard datetrans function can be used in message mapping to change the date and time to a different format.

Examples

Example 1: convert date and time with offset (e.g 2026-03-26T18:00:00+01:00) to UTC

In this case date and time with offset is used from source message and converted to UTC timezone

  • input: date and time with offset (2026-03-26T18:00:00+01:00)
  • pattern: date and time with offset (yyyy-MM-dd'T'HH:mm:ssZ)
  • totimezone: UTC

After converting date and time to UTC, the format is changed to remove milliseconds using datetrans function (format yyyy-MM-dd'T'HH:mm:ss.SSS'Z' to yyyy-MM-dd'T'HH:mm:ss)

Example 2: convert date and time without offset from timezone CET to timezone UTC

In this case date and time is used without offset and timezone has to be added before converting date and time to different timezone

  • input: date and time, timezone is CET and is added using concat function (e.g 2026-02-25 17:59:23 CET)
  • pattern: date and time with offset (yyyy-MM-dd HH:mm:ss ZZZ)
  • totimezone: timezone is UTC

After converting date and time to UTC, format is changed back to original using datetrans function (format yyyy-MM-dd'T'HH:mm:ss.SSS'Z' to yyyy-MM-dd HH:mm:ss)

Example 3: convert date and time from timezone UTC to timezone Europe/Amsterdam

  • input: date and time, timezone is added (e.g 2026-08-27T12:00:00 UTC)
  • pattern: date and time with timezone (yyyy-MM-dd'T'HH:mm:ss ZZZ)
  • totimezone: date and time is set to timezone Europe/Amsterdam

Because offset can be different depending on the day and month for daylight savings time, milliseconds and offset are removed using substring function.

 

References:

  • Timezone conversion for SAP PO: https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-blog-posts-by-members/time-zone-conversion-in-pi-mapping/ba-p/13156873
  • Earlier example of function to convert date and time to different timezone: https://stackoverflow.com/questions/45834774/groovy-date-timezone-formatting
  • Full list of class SimpleDateFormat, used for date and time notation: http://docs.oracle.com/javase/7/docs/api/java/text/SimpleDateFormat.html
  • List of all timezones: http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/List_of_tz_database_time_zones

entry.title: Integrating SAP S/4HANA Plant Maintenance Orders with SAP Field Service Management – Part 1
entry.content:
Introduction

In asset‑intensive industries such as utilities, renewable energy, transportation, and manufacturing, maintenance efficiency plays a decisive role in asset uptime and operational reliability. While SAP S/4HANA Asset Management focuses on planning, structuring, and settling maintenance processes, the actual execution often happens in the field — powered by SAP Field Service Management (FSM).

To avoid inconsistent data, delays, manual work, and visibility gaps, customers increasingly turn to end‑to‑end integration between S/4HANA and FSM. This blog explains the complete process, prerequisites, configuration steps, and execution flow for seamless replication of Maintenance Orders, Activities, Confirmations, Materials, Expenses, and Attachments between the systems.

Key Business Benefits of Integration

  1. Seamless Maintenance Execution: Integration enables a seamless flow from maintenance planning and work order creation in S/4HANA to service execution and confirmation in FSM, ensuring no loss of information across systems.
  2. Real-Time Asset Visibility: Field technicians receive accurate asset master data, functional locations, equipment history, and task instructions directly on their mobile devices, while updates from the field are synchronized back to S/4HANA in real time.
  3. Faster Response and Reduced Downtime: By combining asset intelligence with intelligent scheduling and dispatching, organizations can react quickly to asset issues, minimizing downtime and improving asset availability.
  4. Improved Technician Productivity: FSM provides optimized scheduling, route planning, and mobile execution capabilities, enabling technicians to complete jobs faster and more accurately.
  5. Accurate Cost and Compliance Tracking: Time, spare parts, and service confirmations captured in FSM are transferred back to S/4HANA Asset Management, ensuring accurate cost posting, maintenance history, and regulatory compliance.

Process Overview: S/4HANA to FSM: 

  • In SAP S/4HANA, when a maintenance order is saved, a corresponding service call and activities are automatically created in SAP Field Service Management based on the system statuses defined in the configuration. One or more system statuses can be configured to determine when a maintenance order should be replicated.
  • When a previously replicated maintenance order is saved again in SAP S/4HANA, the service call and associated activities in SAP Field Service Management are automatically updated based on the current system status of the maintenance order and the configured settings.​
  • All the stock & non-stock components from maintenance order operation are replicated to activity as reserved materials.​
  • When a maintenance order is marked as Technically Completed (TECO) in SAP S/4HANA, the status of the corresponding service call in SAP Field Service Management is automatically updated to Technically Completed, provided the statuses are mapped in the configuration. Additionally, all activities within the service call are set to CLOSED.​

Process Overview: FSM to S/4HANA

  • When a service call with type Maintenance is created in SAP Field Service Management, a corresponding maintenance order is created in SAP S/4HANA.​
  • When a service call with type Maintenance is updated in SAP Field Service Management, the corresponding maintenance order is updated in SAP S/4HANA.​
  • When an activity is created in SAP Field Service Management, a corresponding order operation with status Created is created in the relevant maintenance order in SAP S/4HANA.​
  • When an activity of Service Call with type Maintenance is modified in SAP Field Service Management, the Description, Equipment, Duration, Planned Start and End Dates, and Person Responsible are transferred from the activity in SAP Field Service Management to the maintenance order operation in SAP S/4HANA.​
  • When an activity of Service Call with type Maintenance is being assigned to a technician and released in SAP Field Service Management, the corresponding Person Responsible is updated in the Maintenance Order Operation.​
  • After any time effort, materials (planned and unplanned), or expenses are approved in SAP Field Service Management, corresponding documents such as maintenance order confirmations, goods issue/ receipt documents, and G/L documents are created in SAP S/4HANA.​
  • When an attachment is uploaded to a service call or to an activity, the attachment is transferred to SAP S/4HANA.Service call or activity-level attachment replication is supported for maintenance orders and operations as well.​

Sequence of Operations

Prerequisites

  • The minimum SAP S/4HANA version must be 2023 FPS3.
  • Equipments and functional locations are already replicated to SAP FSM.
  • Material and business partners must already be present in SAP FSM
  • Ensure region code in SAP Field Service Management is manually created in SAP S/4HANA following the required naming convention. See more details.
  • CPI iFlows active and configured

Configurations

This section summarizes the technical setup required across S/4HANA, FSM, and CPI.

1 OAuth Config

Use T-code SOAUTH2_CLIENT to configure or view OAuth 2.0 Client Configuration

  • Click on create

  • Give below values for create
    • OAuth 2.0 Client Profile: FSM_PROFILE
    • Configuration: Custom name for your OAuth profile
    • OAuth 2.0 Client ID: Client ID from the FSM

  • Add token endpoint

  • Switch to ‘Client Authentication’ and select ‘Form Fields’. Edit to enter ‘Client Secret’

  • Save and exit the edit mode.

  • Click on ‘Token’ to test the token service. If Access Token comes up as valid then OAuth set up is working

2 Create RFC Connection

Use T-code SOAUTH2_CLIENT to configure or view OAuth 2.0 Client Configuration

  • Select ‘Create’

  • Give appropriate name and select Type G connection

  • Fill details like description and host name under Technical Settings

  • Under Logon & Security, select the OAuth profile created in the previous step

  • Under Logon & Security, change SSL to Active and choose PSE ID. In current system you can choose ANONYM SSL Client (Anonymous)

  • Save and perform connection test. You should get 200 OK response

3 Configure replication in SPRO

  • Configure Replication Parameters

Enter following values relevant for plant maintenance

Scope

Parameter

Value

GLOBAL

RFC

RFC destination created above

GLOBAL

X-Account-ID

Account ID of FSM

GLOBAL

X-Client-ID

Client ID generated in FSM

GLOBAL

X-Client-Version

0.0.1

GLOBAL

X-Company-ID

Company ID of FSM

MAIN_ORDER

S4_FSM_Company_ID

00001

MAIN_ORDER

X-Create-Or-Update

true

MAIN_ORDER

pathConfirmation

/api/fsm-connector/v1/confirmation/${ActivityId}/approvalitem

MAIN_ORDER

pathServiceRoot

/api/fsm-connector/v1/composite-tree/service-calls/

  • Define Plant Specific Order Types

Maintain plant and order type combination that needs to be replicated

S4 Cmp. ID

Type

Plant

00001

YBA1

1710

00001

PM01

1010

  • Define System Status on which maintenance order needs to be replicated.

S4 Cmp. ID

Type

Plant

00001

I0002

Replicate Object

  • Configure Mapping for Service Call Type Codelist

Maintain mapping between of maintenance order type and FSM service call type

  • Configure Mapping for Status Codelist

Maintain mapping between order status in S/4HANA and FSM

  • Configure Mapping for Priority Codelist

Maintain mapping between order priority in S/4HANA and FSM service call priority.

  • Configure Mapping for Problem Type Codelist

Maintain mapping between activity type of Order in S/4HANA and FSM problem type.

  • Configure Region

Ensure region code in SAP Field Service Management is manually created in SAP S/4HANA following the required naming convention based on following setting.

  • Set Default Business Partner (Customer)

Configure the default business partner that will be used in FSM for service call.

  • Configure Mapping for Expense Types

  • Configure for Expense document type item details

4 Set up Service call Type in FSM

      Set up service call type code in FSM settings and configuration by navigating as below in FSM

            Settings and Configuration -> Planning and Dispatching -> Service call -> Service Call Types

5 Set up Service Regions

Create service regions to be used for maintenance order replication. This set up depends on set up done for ‘Configure Region’ in S/4HANA. You can read more details at Create Regions for Maintenance Order Replication | SAP Help Portal

Typically the code will be in the form of WC_<Workcenter Plant>_<Workcenter at Order>
Please maintain at below path in FSM: Settings and Configuration -> Planning and Dispatching -> General Settings -> Service Regions

6 Configure iflows in CPI

Apart from relevant master data iflows, below iflows needs to be configured specifically for maintenance order integration. These iflows replicate updates and confirmation from FSM to S/4HANA.

  • Route Payload from SAP Field Service Management to SAP S/4HANA
    • Route the Maintenance order updates to below maintenance order related iflows.
    • For this it needs only config addition as below to identify correct service call type related to maintenance order

  • Notify Plant Maintenance Order Update from SAP Field Service Management to SAP S/4HANA
    • Replicates the updates like activity assignment release, change in dates.
  • Replicate Plant Maintenance Call from SAP Field Service Management to SAP S/4HANA
    • Replicates newly created service call from FSM to S/4HANA.
  • Update Maintenance Order for New Activity from SAP Field Service Management to SAP S/4HANA
    • Replicates any newly created maintenance activity in FSM as operation to S/4HANA
  • Replicate Confirmed Maintenance from SAP Field Service Management to SAP S/4HANA
    • Replicates the confirmation approved by back office in FSM

entry.title: Integrating SAP S/4HANA Plant Maintenance Orders with SAP Field Service Management – Part 2
entry.content:
Demo

A renewable energy company operates multiple wind farms. Each wind turbine is managed as an equipment object in SAP S/4HANA Asset Management, with defined maintenance plans and performance thresholds.

A wind turbine’s temperature sensor detects that the temperature is higher than normal operating limits and required maintenance.

1 Creation of PM Notification (IW21)

·       Enter T-code IW21 and enter ‘Y1’ as Notification Type then hit Enter or F8

·       Enter Following details to create PM Notification

Short Text/Description: Turbine generator temperature over limit (Add different name to differentiate)

Functional Location, Equipment, Planner Group, Main WorkCtr, Required Endn Date, Final Due Date.

·       Click on ‘Issue Permit’ or Shift+F7

·       Click on ‘Create’ button next to Order

·       Enter YBA1 as value for Order Type and click continue

·       Under Operations tab, add below details to create first operation.

Operation Short Text: Turbine brush replacement

Work: 2 (or any number of hours)

Duration: 2 (or any number of hours)

·       Under Components tab, add below details to add a reserved material for operation.

Component: TURBINE_BRUSH

Requirement Quantity: 3

Storage: 171A

Plant: 1710

Activity: 10

·       You can release the maintenance order by clicking on Release or Ctrl+F1

·       Save the maintenance order and note the order number

2 Verify Service call in FSM

Navigate to service calls under Planning and Dispatching in FSM and filter with the help of External ID

  • Verify details like Equipment, Activities and reserved material under activity

3   Dispatch the activity to technician

    Navigate to Dispatching Board and filter the service call by using Order number as External ID

  • Drag and drop the activity to technician.

  • Release the assignment by clicking on Release Assignments on top right corner while selecting the dragged activity

  • Once released the activity will become visible on the windows app. Do note the activity number to continue with windows app

4 Work and add efforts on windows efforts

  • If app is already logged and initial sync is completed. Do a sync by clicking on synchronization button on top right

  • Once sync completed, you can search with activity code under Activities

  • Click on Work to change status of activity. Confirm any prompts (Do not use CHECKOUT option when available)
  • Click on Reserved Materials to note the use of material

  • Add Efforts by selecting Create Effort under Journal or by selecting Create Effort from Add menu at the bottom right

 

  • Add work time and select the task then save

     

  • Add Expense by selecting Create Expense under Journal or by selecting Create Effort from Add menu at the bottom right

  • Add expense like meals and save

  • Once the journal entries are added you can initiate the sync

5 Verify Time and Material as back office

  • Navigate to Time and Material Journal on the web app under Planning and Dispatching

  • Filter by either activity or service call code

  • Approve each item separately (This is a limitation on test system. In productive landscape bulk approval is possible). For each item provide approval notes

  • Once approved, confirmations will be visible in S4

6 Validate Goods Issue, confirmation and G/L account posting

  • Go to T-code IW33 and enter the order number to display the order
  • From the display of maintenance order, select Document flow or ctrl+F11

     

  • You can check Goods Issue and confirmation posted

  • You can verify more details by double clicking on GI and confirmation entries

  • To view the G/L posting, you can go to T-code FB02 and select Document list or shift+F8

  • Add below parameters for search

Company code: 1710

Fiscal Year: current year

Document Type: SA

Posting Date: Today

  • The exact document number can be verified in AIF Monitor

T-code: /O/AIF/ERR

Namespace: /FSMPM

Interface Name: CONFIRM_IN

Interface Version: 1

Generic Selection: Today

Status Selection: Select All

  • Verify the G/L posting for the amount posted from FSM as expense

7 Create new activity in FSM and replicate as operation

  • Create a new activity under same service call. Important information to add is as below
    • Subject: Example- Backup Battery replacement
      • Planned Duration: change hours to may be 5
      • Region: WC_1710_TATA_ELE
    • Verify the replication by checking the maintenance order (IW33) under operations. Do verify hours entered in FSM being replicated to S4
    • Release the activity to Technician
    • Add an un-planned material by choosing ‘Create Material’ and searching for Turbine Battery
    • Make sure to use material from warehouse 1710/171A

  • You can also add Effort for service hours and sync
  • Approve the material and effort in web app under Time and Material Journal. Do approve the entries one by one
  • Verify the replicated confirmation from document flow on display of maintenance order (IW33)     

8 Create new service call in FSM and replicate as maintenance order (exercise)

  • Create a new service call in FSM by clicking on ‘Create Service Call’ in service call screen under planning and dispatching
  • Give below details and save to create a service call

Subject: Any subject of choice

Type: Maintenance

Problem Type: Any (You can choose Mechanical)

Responsible: Technician Name

Customer: Default customer

  • Note the created service call code. Close and open the service call to check the updated external ID

  • In S/4, Navigate to display maintenance order (IW33) to verify the order replicated. You can create operations and add components to verify replication further

9 Add attachment from FSM

  • On the service call created in last step, add an attachment from Attachments tab
    • Adding attachment triggers replication, no extra step need to confirm the attachment addition in FSM
  • Verify if attached replicated to maintenance order by verifying under ‘Additional Data’ -> ‘Document Assignments’

entry.title: SAP Process Orchestration to SAP Integration Suite Migration - Alert Rules via SAP Cloud ALM
entry.content:
Objective

The goal of this blog is to explain how to migrate your alert rules from SAP Process Orchestration and set up proactive monitoring for SAP Integration Suite exclusively using SAP Cloud ALM. We will cover the end-to-end setup including:

  • SAP BTP Cockpit configuration and endpoint creation.
  • Service and Endpoint configuration within SAP Cloud ALM.
  • Setting up alerts, filters, and event actions in Integration & Exception Monitoring.

Understanding the Transition to SAP Cloud ALM

When migrating your integration scenarios from SAP Process Orchestration to SAP Integration Suite, recreating your alerting mechanisms is a critical step. By using SAP Cloud ALM for your monitoring, you establish a connection point that improves visibility for all connected systems and ensures that integration errors are automatically detected and communicated.

Step 1: SAP BTP Cockpit Configuration

To connect SAP Cloud ALM with SAP Integration Suite, you must first create an endpoint in the SAP BTP Cockpit capable of retrieving Message Logs and Artifact Health data.

  • Navigate to your Subaccount from the Global Account in SAP BTP Cockpit.
  • Go to Services -> Instances and Subscriptions and choose Create.
  • Select the Process Integration Runtime Service and choose the integration-flow plan.

 

  • Add the following roles to the instance: MonitoringDataRead and MonitoringArtifactsDeploy.

 

  • After creating the instance, open it to create a Service Key.
  • Provide a descriptive name, set the Key Type to Client ID/Secret, and save the key in JSON format securely, as it will be required in later steps.

Step 2: SAP Cloud ALM Configuration

With the BTP endpoint prepared, the next phase is establishing the connection in SAP Cloud ALM. The user performing this needs specific roles: x_landscape_Administrator and x_landscape_SecurityAdministrator.

  • Access Administration and navigate to Landscape Management.

 

  • Under Services & Systems, you can attempt to automatically import your service by navigating to Configuration -> Import of Subscribed SAP Services.

 

 

  • If an automatic import isn't possible (which typically happens if the daily synchronization job hasn't run yet, the service is registered under a different customer number, or local access controls restrict your visibility), you can manually add a New Service. To do this, define parameters such as the Name and the System Number. To find your System Number, log into SAP for Me, navigate to 'Portfolio & Products' > 'My Product Portfolio', click on your specific product, and look under the 'Systems' tab for the tenant's unique identifier. Finally, select the Service Type (SAP Integration Suite) and input the Root URL / Logon URL that you copied from your saved Service Key.

 

  • Once the service is created, select Add in the Endpoints section and paste the Service Key details you saved earlier to automatically fill the parameters, then click Check Connection.

 

 

Step 3: Setting Up Alerts and Notifications

Now that the external systems are connected, you can configure the actual alert rules using the Integration & Exception Monitoring section in Operations.

To perform this, users need roles such as ops_im_IntegrationArchitect, ops_im_IntegrationOwner, or x_calm_GlobalAdministrator.

  • Select your newly configured service from the Managed Components list (under Services) and click Apply.

 

 

  • Access Edit Configuration for the service and turn on Data Collection to begin gathering monitoring data.

     

 

 

 

  • Navigate to Events to add the specific events used for alerts.

 

 

  • For individual Integration Flow exceptions, add an event and set the Event Name to "Erroneous CPI Message Detected (Single)". To monitor specific messages or restrict events to certain integration flows, you can create custom filters. In the Filters tab, you can define filter parameters for specific integration flows. For example, you can limit the alert to a single interface by specifying its Artifact Name or Artifact ID, or include multiple interfaces by providing a list of names. Furthermore, you can use powerful combinations by pairing the Artifact Name with specific Statuses or Error Messages (e.g., only triggering the alert if 'iFlow_A' fails with an HTTP 500 error).

 

  • You can define specific parameters (like triggering the event for errors older than 5 minutes) and use the Filters tab to restrict the events to specific integration flows.

 

Post-Configuration Activities: Event Actions and Customizing Output

Just like manual fine-tuning steps in a interface migration, your event routing will need a few final touches:

 

  • Select verified recipients from the Recipient section. If your recipients aren't listed, add them by going to SAP Cloud ALM > Operations > Notification Management, clicking the '+' icon, and entering the target email addresses separated by commas.

 

  • When an error matches your defined parameters and filters, the system will automatically trigger an email notification containing key details like the integration artifact, message ID, status, and timestamp.

 

  • For single erroneous messages, you can highly customize the information displayed in the email up to 7 fields by choosing the appropriate attributes on the Grouping tab during Event configuration.

 

Conclusion

Migrating your alert rules to SAP Cloud ALM simplifies the monitoring process while guaranteeing that your SAP Integration Suite landscape remains highly visible. By establishing a secure BTP endpoint and defining granular event filters and notification actions, you can ensure reliable, scalable, and proactive error handling for all your newly migrated integration interfaces.

For additional questions related to the SAP Process Orchestration to SAP Integration Suite migration, see FAQ – Migration from SAP Process Orchestration to SAP Integration Suite.

entry.title: Currency Conversion Integration using SAP CPI with Groovy and XSLT
entry.content:
Introduction

In many real-world integration scenarios, applications need to handle data across different currencies. Whether it’s for reporting, billing, or analytics, converting currency values accurately and in real time is an important requirement.

In this blog, I’ll walk through how I built a simple and practical currency conversion integration using SAP Cloud Integration (CPI). The iFlow accepts a JSON request, calls an external API to fetch exchange rates, processes the response using Groovy, and transforms the output into a clean, readable format using XSLT. Finally, the result is sent as an email, making it easy for end users to view.

 

Integration Flow Overview

This integration flow performs the following:

  • Accepts a currency conversion request in JSON format
  • Calls an external exchange rate API
  • Converts the source amount into target currency
  • Transforms the response into a readable HTML format
  • Sends the final output via Email

 

Integration Flow Diagram

HTTPS Sender → JSON to XML Converter → Content Modifier (Extract Properties) → Request Reply (External API Call) → Groovy Script (Processing) → Content Modifier (Build XML) → XSLT Mapping (HTML Output) → Email Receiver

Below is the iFlow implemented for this scenario:

The iFlow demonstrates message splitting, dynamic routing, and CSV transformation before sending data to respective receivers.

Input Payload Structure

Step 1: JSON to XML Conversion

The incoming JSON payload is converted into XML to enable XPath-based extraction in subsequent steps.

Step 2: Extracting Properties 

Using Content Modifier, the following fields are extracted:

 

 
 
These are stored as exchange properties for reuse.

Step 3: External API Call 

A Request Reply step is used to call the exchange rate API dynamically:

 Step 4: Groovy Script Processing

The API response is processed using Groovy to extract relevant fields and format the converted amount.

 Step 5: Building Output XML

A structured XML is created:

 Step 6: XSLT Transformation

The XML is transformed into an HTML table using XSLT to make the output readable.

 Step 7: Email Notification

 The final formatted result is sent via Email using the Mail adapter.

 

 Challenges Faced 

  • Handling API authentication issues
  • Parsing dynamic JSON responses
  • Resolving null pointer exceptions
  • Ensuring correct data formatting

 

Key Learnings

  • Importance of validating API responses
  • Effective use of Groovy for JSON processing
  • Combining XML and XSLT for formatting
  • Designing efficient CPI iFlows

 

Conclusion

This use case demonstrates how SAP CPI can be leveraged to build real-time integrations involving external APIs, data transformation, and user-friendly output delivery.

This implementation helped me better understand how to design flexible and maintainable integration flows, especially when dealing with real-world requirements.

I hope this experience helps others who are working on similar scenarios in SAP Cloud Integration.

Happy to hear feedback or suggestions from the community.

entry.title: Cloud Integration Pipeline - Datastore Extension UI
entry.content:
⚠️  Disclaimer: The UI described in this blog post is not an official product by SAP. If you are interested in using the UI, you can contact @lisarizzo or @mike_sibler.  It will soon also be available as an SAP Open Source project.  

 

Purpose and background

The pipeline in Cloud Integration is a well-established framework for asynchronous integrations. A blog post to explain the starting points for the pipeline can be found here.

In addition to the pipeline, we offer the datastore extension, which simplifies error handling via automatic retry jobs and APIs for resending and cancelling messages. For details on the datastore extension, please refer to this blog post. The datastore extension package provides automatic restart capabilities with Restart Jobs that are based on pre-delivered IFlows. In addition, it provides on-demand operations that require the use of API client tools such as Postman or Bruno. This is technical and error-prone to handle from a user perspective.

The UI introduced in this blog post is designed to provide a simplified and user-friendly approach to access these on-demand APIs.

On a technical level, it is relevant to note that the UI does not call Cloud Integration APIs directly. It calls IFlows of the Datastore Extension package, which are on-demand API Operations (Manage Retries and Manage Datastore Entries). It triggers a corresponding IFlow and operation and then displays the response.

This blog article introduces and explains the functionality of the Cloud Integration Pipeline Datastore Extension UI.

 

Overview of key features

The current version of the UI provides these capabilities, which are shown again in more detail in the “How to use the UI” section.

  • Visual overview of all available datastores relevant for the pipeline reprocessing, their messages and overdue messages
  • Search and filter capabilities to quickly locate specific datastores
  • Visual overview of all messages in each datastore
  • Search and filter capabilities to quickly locate specific messages
  • Action to restart messages from the UI
  • Action to move messages to another datastore or a no-retry datastore when located in a retry datastore
  • Action to delete/cancel messages 
  • Detailed view of message content (error, headers, payload)
  • Possibility to perform batch actions to restart, move, cancel several messages at once

 

How to use the UI

Datastore overview

The first screen of the UI shows a table with all the datastores, the number of messages, and the number of overdue messages. You can also search and filter the table for specific information. 

Datastore detail view and overview of messages

The second screen shows details of the selected datastore and contains a table with all the messages. 

One or more messages can be selected in the table and moved to another datastore or to a no-retry datastore. The messages can also be restarted or deleted from here.  

There are sorting capabilities available for the datastore entries table. They can be found behind the gear icon ⚙️. For example, the table can be sorted by the number of restarts and the timestamp of error, ascending or descending. 

There is also a filter hidden behind the same gear icon ⚙️.

Message detail view

When clicking on one of the rows in the datastore entries table the user is directed to a third screen with details about the error, headers and payload. If, after inspecting, the user decided what to do with the message it is also possible to perform the same move, delete and restart actions from this screen with the buttons in the top right corner.

 

Outlook & how to implement the UI

This project will soon be available on the SAP GitHub. Until then, for information on how to implement the Datastore Extension UI, please reach out to lisa.rizzo@sap.com or mike.sibler@sap.com . Once the project is published on the SAP GitHub as an open-source project, you can find the link to the repository and a detailed implementation guide here in this blog post.
